2000.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.2% of GDP in 2000. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2001.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.0% of GDP in 2001. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2002.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 2.8% of GDP in 2002. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2003.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 2.4% of GDP in 2003. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2004.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.0% of GDP in 2004. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2005.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.5% of GDP in 2005. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2006.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.8% of GDP in 2006. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2007.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 3.5% of GDP in 2007. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2008.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 2.8% of GDP in 2008. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2009.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 4.2% of GDP in 2009. Budget supported by external aid flows and donor budget support; debt manageable.

2010.assessment:
Public debt was approximately 28% of GDP in 2010, in the low-debt category.
Mali benefited from HIPC debt relief completion in 2003 which reduced the
debt burden significantly. IMF program (ECF) maintaining fiscal discipline.
Pre-crisis baseline with strong macroeconomic management under ATT government.
Gold and cotton export revenues supporting fiscal position.

2011.assessment:
Public debt approximately 29% of GDP in 2011. Still in the low-debt category,
marginally higher than 2010. IMF ECF program maintaining fiscal discipline.
Pre-crisis baseline, strong economic performance. MNLA rebellion had not yet
begun (started January 2012), so no fiscal emergency spending required.

2012.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 32% of GDP in 2012. The coup and crisis
caused revenue shortfalls as economic activity contracted. ECOWAS sanctions
and international aid suspension reduced budget support. Emergency military
spending increased. IMF program suspended after coup. Debt crossing into the
manageable range but still below concerning levels.

2013.assessment:
Public debt approximately 32% of GDP in 2013. Post-crisis recovery with aid
flows resuming. IMF ECF program restored after elections. Operation Serval
(French-funded) reduced direct Mali military burden. Aid disbursements from
IMF and donors supporting budget. Debt stabilizing at manageable levels.

2014.assessment:
Public debt approximately 33% of GDP in 2014. Gradual increase driven by
security expenditure needs and infrastructure investment. IMF ECF program
on track maintaining fiscal discipline. Algiers Peace Process beginning.
Defense spending elevated due to continued FAMA operations against jihadists.

2015.assessment:
Public debt approximately 36% of GDP in 2015. Continued gradual increase.
Security expenditure maintained. Commodity price declines (gold, cotton)
reduced revenues. CFA franc depreciation against USD increased dollar-denominated
debt service costs marginally. IMF ECF program continuing. Algiers Accord
signed June 2015 but DDR costs expected.

2016.assessment:
Public debt approximately 36% of GDP in 2016. Stable at prior year levels.
Economy recovering. Gold revenues improving with higher prices. IMF ECF
program supporting fiscal discipline. Elevated defense spending maintained.
Debt remaining manageable with IMF program guardrails.

2017.assessment:
Public debt approximately 37% of GDP in 2017. Gradual modest increase.
New borrowing for infrastructure and security expenditure. IMF ECF program
continuing to maintain fiscal discipline. G5 Sahel funding arrangements
partially offsetting security spending. Manageable debt trajectory.

2018.assessment:
Public debt approximately 38% of GDP in 2018. Continued gradual increase.
Election year (Keïta re-elected August 2018) with some spending pressures.
Worsening security requiring increased FAMA spending. IMF ECF program
continued providing fiscal anchor. Gold sector performing supporting revenues.

2019.assessment:
Public debt crossed 40% of GDP in 2019. Security expenditure continuing to
rise as situation in Mopti and Gao deteriorated. Infrastructure borrowing
adding to stock. IMF ECF program maintained fiscal discipline. Despite
higher debt ratio, still within manageable range given strong GDP growth.
Political protests against Keïta government building.

2020.assessment:
Public debt surged to approximately 50% of GDP in 2020. COVID-19 emergency
spending and revenue shortfalls drove fiscal deterioration. August 2020 coup
suspended international budget support. ECOWAS sanctions constrained revenues.
IMF emergency COVID financing partially mitigated the shock. The debt ratio
approached the upper bound of the manageable range.

2021.assessment:
Public debt approximately 53% of GDP in 2021. Continued deterioration. May 2021
second coup maintained ECOWAS sanctions and international isolation. IMF program
suspended, removing fiscal discipline guardrails. Security spending rising with
Wagner transition costs. Debt approaching the upper bound of the manageable
category; fiscal sustainability under increasing pressure.

2022.assessment:
Public debt approximately 55% of GDP in 2022. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January
2022 but junta rule with Wagner forces creating fiscal pressures. Wagner
deployment estimated to cost $10-15M/month. Russia-Ukraine commodity shock
increased import costs. IMF program remained suspended. Debt approaching
upper limit of manageable range with fiscal trajectory uncertain under junta.

2023.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 5.5% of GDP in 2023. Junta governance with reduced donor support and security spending drove larger deficits.

2024.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 5.8% of GDP in 2024. Junta governance with reduced donor support and security spending drove larger deficits.

2025.assessment: Mali maintained a fiscal deficit of approximately 6.0% of GDP in 2025. Junta governance with reduced donor support and security spending drove larger deficits.

2000.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 231 in 2000 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2001.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 247 in 2001 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2002.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 267 in 2002 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2003.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 313 in 2003 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2004.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 338 in 2004 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2005.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 381 in 2005 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2006.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 440 in 2006 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2007.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 520 in 2007 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2008.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 637 in 2008 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2009.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 622 in 2009 (current prices). Democratic governance and gold mining drove steady growth in this period.

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita was approximately USD 693 in 2010, below what would be the
later period peak. Mali remained one of the poorest countries in the world.
Gold mining and cotton exports supported growth but deep structural poverty
persisted. The Tuareg rebellion was not yet active; political stability
under President Touré (ATT) maintained.

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to approximately USD 733 in 2011. Good cotton and
gold production supported growth. No major conflict events yet — the MNLA
rebellion began in January 2012. This year represents the pre-crisis peak
before the 2012 multiple shock (rebellion + coup + territorial loss).

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to approximately USD 694 in 2012 (down ~5% from 2011).
The Tuareg/jihadist rebellion and March 2012 coup devastated economic activity.
Northern Mali — containing major gold and economic assets — was under rebel
and jihadist control for much of the year. ECOWAS sanctions further constrained
economic activity. International aid largely suspended during the political crisis.

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to approximately USD 734 in 2013. French Operation
Serval (January 2013) drove jihadists from major northern cities, restoring
northern economic activity. Aid flows resumed. MINUSMA UN peacekeeping mission
deployed from July 2013. Elections held, restoring civilian government under
President Keïta (IBK). Agricultural production recovered.

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 744 in 2014. Slow but steady economic
recovery. Continued jihadist insurgency in the north constrained economic
activity in rural areas. Algiers Peace Process for northern Mali begun.
Gold mining sector partially disrupted by insecurity. IMF program continuing.

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita dipped slightly to approximately USD 727 in 2015. Global
commodity price declines (gold, cotton) reduced export values. CFA franc
depreciation against USD reduced current-dollar values. Algiers Accord
signed June 2015. Continued insecurity in the center and north of the country.

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita increased to approximately USD 797 in 2016. Economic recovery
strengthening. Gold prices recovering. Agricultural production improved.
Algiers Accord implementation proceeding (slowly). Violence spreading from
the north toward the center (Mopti region). IMF ECF program maintaining
macroeconomic stability.

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 821 in 2017. Solid economic growth despite
ongoing insecurity. Gold mining expanding. Operation Barkhane (French CT)
and MINUSMA providing security umbrella. G5 Sahel Joint Force established.
Insecurity spreading to central Mali creating displacement and disrupting
the agricultural heartland.

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 864 in 2018. Continued economic growth
despite worsening security. Jihadist attacks spreading throughout central
Mali (Mopti region). Elections held (Keïta re-elected in disputed August
2018 election). Gold sector expanding. IMF ECF program continued.

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately USD 909 in 2019 — the period peak.
Strong agricultural growth and gold sector performance drove gains. Despite
worsening security across the center and north, the formal economy (Bamako
centered) continued to grow. IMF program continued. Political protests
against Keïta government beginning.

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to approximately USD 869 in 2020 — a 4% decline from
peak. COVID-19 disrupted the economy. August 2020 coup (Colonel Goïta ousting
President Keïta) caused ECOWAS sanctions and political uncertainty. International
aid partially suspended after coup. Security crisis worsening across the country.
Gold sector maintained but other sectors contracted.

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 885 in 2021. May 2021 second coup (Goïta
again) deepened international isolation. ECOWAS maintained sanctions. IMF
program suspended. International aid cut further. Security situation
deteriorating. Despite political crisis, gold sector performed and agricultural
production was average.

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 896 in 2022. Junta government with Wagner
forces replacing French Barkhane. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January 2022 after
transition timeline agreed. However, French expulsion, MINUSMA difficulties,
and Wagner presence created ongoing uncertainty. Gold sector remained the
economic anchor. Security across most of country deteriorating rapidly.

2023.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 920 in 2023 (current prices). Under military junta, economic management suffered from sanctions, reduced aid, and security costs.

2024.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 950 in 2024 (current prices). Under military junta, economic management suffered from sanctions, reduced aid, and security costs.

2025.assessment: Mali's GDP per capita was approximately USD 980 in 2025 (current prices). Under military junta, economic management suffered from sanctions, reduced aid, and security costs.

2000.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 0.7% in 2000. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2001.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 5.2% in 2001. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2002.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 5.0% in 2002. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2003.assessment: Inflation in Mali was -1.3% in 2003. Deflation in 2003-2004 reflected CFA franc appreciation and low food prices.

2004.assessment: Inflation in Mali was -3.1% in 2004. Deflation in 2003-2004 reflected CFA franc appreciation and low food prices.

2005.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 6.4% in 2005. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2006.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 1.5% in 2006. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2007.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 1.5% in 2007. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2008.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 9.1% in 2008. Elevated inflation driven by food and fuel prices.

2009.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 2.2% in 2009. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2010.assessment:
Inflation was 1.3% in 2010, low and stable thanks to the CFA franc zone
(WAEMU member), where the BCEAO maintains price stability through the
euro peg. Mali's food production was satisfactory in 2010 contributing
to low food price inflation.

2011.assessment:
Inflation rose to 3.1% in 2011, slightly above the 3% CFA zone target.
Global commodity price increases (food, oil) drove modest price pressures.
Still well within the moderate range. MNLA rebellion had not yet begun
(started January 2012), so no conflict-related supply disruptions.

2012.assessment:
Inflation jumped to 5.3% in 2012 — the highest in the priority period.
The coup (March 2012) and loss of northern territory disrupted food supply
chains. ECOWAS sanctions restricted goods movement. Displacement of
northern populations strained southern food markets. Supply disruptions
were the primary inflation driver.

2013.assessment:
Deflation of -0.6% in 2013. Post-crisis supply restoration (Operation
Serval, ECOWAS sanctions lifted) flooded markets with previously restricted
goods. Good agricultural harvest. Price normalization after 2012 spike.
CFA zone deflationary tendencies when supply shocks resolve.

2014.assessment:
Inflation was 0.9% in 2014, returning to a low stable level. Good
agricultural production. Global food price moderation. CFA franc stability
maintained. IMF program on track. Economy normalizing from 2012 crisis.

2015.assessment:
Inflation was 1.4% in 2015. Stable and low. Global commodity price
declines reduced input costs. CFA franc peg to euro provided monetary
anchor. Algiers Accord signed but implementation stalling. Economy
growing despite continued insecurity.

2016.assessment:
Deflation of -1.8% in 2016. Global commodity price declines feeding
through into consumer prices. CFA franc appreciated modestly in real
terms reducing import costs. Food prices declined. No supply shock
inflation. CFA zone deflationary pressure from European monetary policy.

2017.assessment:
Inflation was 1.8% in 2017. Commodity price recovery (food, oil) from
2016 lows. Still within low range. Economy performing well despite insecurity.
Elections held in 2018 (campaigns in 2017). IMF ECF program disbursements
continuing.

2018.assessment:
Inflation was 1.7% in 2018. Stable and low. Election year (Keïta re-elected
August 2018). Economy growing. Gold sector performing well. CFA franc peg
maintained stability. Security crisis in Mopti and Gao regions continuing
but not yet feeding through to national inflation.

2019.assessment:
Significant deflation of -3.0% in 2019. Unusual deflationary episode
driven by global commodity price movements and good agricultural harvest.
CFA franc strength contributed. Food prices declined sharply in 2019.
Despite political tensions (protests against IBK government beginning),
monetary stability maintained through the WAEMU framework.

2020.assessment:
Inflation was 0.5% in 2020. COVID-19 and the August 2020 coup reduced
demand and economic activity. Supply disruptions partially offset by
demand collapse. CFA peg maintained stability through the political
transition. ECOWAS sanctions did not dramatically affect consumer prices
given the gold export dominance.

2021.assessment:
Inflation rose to 3.8% in 2021 — above target. May 2021 second coup
and ECOWAS sanctions created supply disruptions. Global commodity prices
recovering post-COVID drove food inflation. IMF program suspended.
Wagner Group beginning to replace Barkhane created uncertainty. Political
isolation contributing to supply chain inefficiencies.

2022.assessment:
Inflation spiked to 9.7% in 2022 — the highest in the period. Global
commodity price shock (Russia-Ukraine war) drove food and energy prices.
Mali's isolation under junta rule (Wagner, French expulsion) disrupted
supply chains. ECOWAS sanctions (later lifted) had already stressed
import capacity. Despite CFA franc peg, imported inflation overwhelmed
the monetary anchor.

2023.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 3.8% in 2023. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2024.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 4.5% in 2024. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2025.assessment: Inflation in Mali was 5.0% in 2025. Moderate inflation consistent with WAEMU regional monetary policy.

2000.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 62% in 2000. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2001.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 63% in 2001. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2002.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 58% in 2002. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2003.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 57% in 2003. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2004.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 64% in 2004. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2005.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 64% in 2005. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2006.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 67% in 2006. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2007.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 70% in 2007. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2008.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 74% in 2008. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2009.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 60% in 2009. Gold exports and cotton exports dominated; WAEMU free trade framework.

2010.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 58% of GDP in 2010. Mali is a landlocked
country heavily dependent on gold and cotton exports and imports of petroleum,
machinery, and food. Trade flows via Senegal and Cote d'Ivoire ports. The
WAEMU (UEMOA) regional trade framework facilitates integration with coastal
neighbors. Pre-crisis baseline.

2011.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 58% of GDP in 2011. Stable pre-crisis baseline.
Gold exports performing well. Cotton sector recovering from prior-year difficulties.
No major disruption to trade routes yet — MNLA rebellion began January 2012.
WAEMU trade facilitation maintained.

2012.assessment:
Trade openness fell sharply to approximately 51% of GDP in 2012. The coup (March
2012) and ECOWAS sanctions restricted trade. Loss of northern Mali territory
disrupted artisanal gold and agricultural exports from those regions. International
aid and import financing partially collapsed. Trade via Cote d'Ivoire and Senegal
disrupted by sanctions and political uncertainty.

2013.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to approximately 56% of GDP in 2013. Operation Serval
restored northern trade routes. ECOWAS sanctions lifted. Aid flows resumed bringing
import capacity. Elections and civilian government under Keïta normalizing trade
relationships. Gold sector restoring export flows.

2014.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 55% of GDP in 2014. Modest recovery but below
pre-crisis level. Continued insecurity in the north and center constraining
full trade restoration. Gold mining sector providing export base. IMF ECF
program on track supporting trade finance.

2015.assessment:
Trade openness declined to approximately 52% of GDP in 2015. Global commodity
price declines (gold, cotton) reduced export values. CFA franc depreciation
against USD reduced current dollar export values. Algiers Accord signed June 2015
but implementation stalling. Ongoing insecurity constraining trade volumes.

2016.assessment:
Trade openness recovered slightly to approximately 54% of GDP in 2016. Gold
price recovery supporting export value. Economy growing. Algiers Accord
implementation proceeding slowly. Violence spreading south toward Mopti
but not yet significantly affecting primary trade corridors.

2017.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 56% of GDP in 2017. Gold sector expanding and
supporting exports. Economy performing well. G5 Sahel Force established providing
some security for trade routes. Central Mali insecurity increasingly disrupting
agricultural trade in Mopti and Segou regions.

2018.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 57% of GDP in 2018. Continued growth driven by
gold and agricultural exports. Election year (Keïta re-elected August 2018).
Security situation in Mopti and central Mali worsening but Bamako-centered
formal economy and gold sector trade continuing.

2019.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 58% of GDP in 2019. GDP per capita peaked this
year reflecting strong overall economic performance. Gold sector driving export
growth. Agricultural exports benefiting from good harvest. Political protests
against Keïta government building but not yet disrupting trade.

2020.assessment:
Trade openness fell to approximately 53% of GDP in 2020. COVID-19 disrupted
global supply chains. August 2020 coup caused ECOWAS sanctions freezing some
trade. Services and tourism trade collapsed. Gold exports maintained providing
the primary trade anchor. Import financing partially constrained by political
uncertainty.

2021.assessment:
Trade openness partially recovered to approximately 54% of GDP in 2021. May 2021
second coup maintained international isolation. ECOWAS sanctions continued. IMF
program suspended. Global trade recovery post-COVID partially offset by political
isolation. Gold sector remained the dominant export.

2022.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 56% of GDP in 2022. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January
2022 restoring trade access. However, French expulsion, MINUSMA operational
difficulties, and Wagner presence created ongoing uncertainty. Russia-Ukraine
war raised import costs (fuel, food) elevating the import side. Gold exports
maintained but supply chain inefficiencies from junta isolation persisted.

2023.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 45% in 2023. Reduced trade due to security crisis and junta governance.

2024.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 42% in 2024. Reduced trade due to security crisis and junta governance.

2025.assessment: Mali's trade openness (exports + imports as % GDP) was approximately 40% in 2025. Reduced trade due to security crisis and junta governance.

2000.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.0% in 2000. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2001.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.1% in 2001. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2002.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.2% in 2002. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2003.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.1% in 2003. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2004.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.0% in 2004. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2005.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 6.9% in 2005. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2006.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 6.8% in 2006. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2007.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 6.6% in 2007. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2008.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 6.5% in 2008. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2009.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 6.5% in 2009. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Cotton and gold economy provided rural employment.

2010.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 8.0% in 2010 per ILO modeled estimates. Mali's
labor market is dominated by informal employment, subsistence agriculture,
and artisanal mining. Formal sector unemployment understates true labor
market stress. Pre-crisis baseline under stable ATT government.

2011.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 8.1% in 2011. Stable pre-crisis baseline. MNLA
rebellion had not yet started (began January 2012). Cotton and gold sectors
supporting employment. Agriculture was the primary employer with subsistence
farming predominant in the rural population (>70% of labor force).

2012.assessment:
Unemployment rose to approximately 8.5% in 2012 as the coup and territorial
loss disrupted economic activity. Northern Mali's loss — containing significant
economic activity including artisanal gold and agriculture — displaced workers
southward. ECOWAS sanctions restricted trade and formal sector employment.
The ILO modeled estimate likely understates the spike in informal unemployment.

2013.assessment:
Unemployment eased slightly to approximately 8.3% in 2013. Operation Serval
(January 2013) restored northern Mali, allowing some displaced workers to
return. Civil government under Keïta elected. Aid flows and MINUSMA deployment
provided some employment stimulus. Agricultural recovery supported rural employment.

2014.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 8.2% in 2014. Gradual recovery but ongoing insecurity
in the north and center constraining full employment normalization. Algiers Peace
Process underway. Gold mining employment partially recovered. IMF ECF program
supporting macroeconomic stability.

2015.assessment:
Unemployment returned to approximately 8.0% in 2015. Economic recovery continuing
despite ongoing jihadist activity. Algiers Accord signed June 2015. Agricultural
employment stable. Youth unemployment remained structurally elevated above the
headline figure, particularly in urban areas and the north.

2016.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 7.9% in 2016. Modest improvement as economy grew.
Gold sector expansion providing formal employment. Construction sector in Bamako
growing. However, violence spreading to central Mali (Mopti region) disrupting
agricultural employment in the country's most productive farming zone.

2017.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 7.8% in 2017. Economy performing well at the aggregate
level. G5 Sahel Force and Barkhane operations providing some security employment.
Insecurity spreading to central Mali creating displacement and disrupting
agricultural employment for the increasingly affected farming population.

2018.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 7.7% in 2018. Election year (Keïta re-elected August
2018). Economy growing. Gold sector expanding providing formal jobs. Security
deterioration in Mopti displacing hundreds of thousands but formal sector and
Bamako economy continuing to absorb some labor.

2019.assessment:
Unemployment stable at approximately 7.7% in 2019. Economy at GDP per capita
peak. Gold sector and agriculture performing well at aggregate level. Growing
political protests against Keïta government but unemployment not a primary
driver of protests (governance and security were primary grievances).

2020.assessment:
Unemployment rose to approximately 8.2% in 2020. COVID-19 reduced economic
activity and informal employment. August 2020 coup caused ECOWAS sanctions
and political uncertainty disrupting private sector employment. Tourism and
services collapsed. Gold sector partially maintained.

2021.assessment:
Unemployment elevated at approximately 8.4% in 2021. May 2021 second coup
deepened international isolation. ECOWAS sanctions continued. IMF program
suspended. Aid reductions affecting employment in NGO/development sector.
Security deterioration across the country disrupting agricultural employment
for growing internally displaced populations.

2022.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 8.5% in 2022. Junta rule with Wagner forces.
ECOWAS sanctions lifted January 2022 but French expulsion and ongoing insecurity
created economic uncertainty. Inflation at 9.7% eroded real wages significantly.
Gold sector remained the main formal employer. Security deterioration across most
of the country continued to displace agricultural workers.

2023.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.2% in 2023. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Security crisis increased displacement and informal sector distress.

2024.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.5% in 2024. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Security crisis increased displacement and informal sector distress.

2025.assessment: Formal unemployment in Mali was approximately 7.8% in 2025. Subsistence agriculture and informal trade absorb most of the workforce. Urban unemployment in Bamako was substantially higher. Security crisis increased displacement and informal sector distress.

2000.assessment: Mali maintained full diplomatic relations in 2000. As a model West African democracy hosting international organizations and maintaining active development partnerships with France, the US, and multilateral institutions, Mali had excellent international standing. The country participated actively in ECOWAS, AU, and UN frameworks.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports, Mali 2000

The country participated actively in ECOWAS, AU, and UN frameworks.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports, Mali 2005

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports, Mali 2009

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2010. Mali was a recognized democracy with full
membership in ECOWAS, AU, UN, Francophone community (OIF), and all major
international bodies. Embassies from France, US, Germany, EU, Algeria, Mauritania,
Senegal and many others maintained in Bamako. Normal aid relationships with IMF,
World Bank, bilateral donors.

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2011. Same as 2010 — Mali in good standing with
all international bodies. Pre-crisis year. No diplomatic complications from
MNLA formation (October 2011). Full embassy presence maintained.

2012.assessment:
Significant isolation in 2012 following coup. ECOWAS suspended Mali. AU suspended
Mali from all activities. Some embassies reduced staff or temporarily closed.
US cut non-humanitarian aid. EU suspended budget support. International community
refused to recognize junta. Transitional framework agreed with ECOWAS partially
normalized some relations. Worst diplomatic isolation since independence.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali diplomatic isolation post-2012 coup

2013.assessment:
Diplomatic integration substantially restored in 2013. Operation Serval (French
intervention January 2013) demonstrated major power engagement. Elections July-
August 2013 restored civilian government and full diplomatic recognition.
MINUSMA deployed July 2013 with broad international support. ECOWAS and AU
re-admitted Mali. Aid relationships normalized. France, US, and other allies
increased engagement.

2014.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration in 2014. Civilian government under Keïta in good
international standing. Algiers Peace Process international involvement. MINUSMA
active. Barkhane formally launched August 2014. IMF ECF on track. G5 Sahel
framework emerging. Full embassy presence. Aid relationships normal.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2015. Algiers Accord (June 2015) signed with
international mediation (UN, ECOWAS, Algeria, EU). Major international presence
with MINUSMA, Barkhane, diplomatic missions. G5 Sahel developing. IMF ECF
active. Post-Radisson Blu attack November 2015 showed continued Western engagement.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2016. Mali in good standing with all international
partners. French Barkhane active. MINUSMA largest UN mission. G5 Sahel
institutionalizing. IMF ECF continuing. All major embassies present in Bamako.
Growing international attention to Sahel security crisis.

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2017. G5 Sahel Joint Force formally launched.
France committed additional troops. MINUSMA maintaining large presence. IMF
ECF active. All major bilateral and multilateral relationships functioning.
International concern about Sahel security driving increased diplomatic
engagement, not isolation.

2018.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration in 2018. Disputed elections generated diplomatic
concern but no major rupture. International community accepted (if reluctantly)
Keïta's re-election. MINUSMA and Barkhane continuing. IMF ECF continuing.
G5 Sahel active. France committed to Sahel counterterrorism.

2019.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration in 2019. Security deterioration generating
international concern but driving more engagement, not less. Alliance for
Sahel formed. G5 Sahel receiving international funding. MINUSMA and Barkhane
active. IMF program continuing. Pre-coup civilian government in full standing.

2020.assessment:
Partial isolation in 2020 following August coup. ECOWAS suspended Mali.
AU suspended Mali. US cut military cooperation. EU suspended budget support.
However, France and Western allies maintained some engagement for counterterrorism
purposes. MINUSMA continued. Transitional framework (ECOWAS mediation) partially
restored some international engagement by end of year.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali diplomatic partial isolation post-Aug 2020 coup

2021.assessment:
Significant isolation in 2021. Second coup May 2021 deepened isolation. ECOWAS
maintained suspension. IMF program suspended. France increasingly questioning
military engagement. Wagner Group negotiations indicating geopolitical shift away
from Western partners. Aid organizations reducing presence. International
community frustrated with junta's extended transition timeline.

2022.assessment:
Significant isolation in 2022. France expelled February 2022. ECOWAS
comprehensive sanctions January-July 2022. MINUSMA access restricted. Wagner
presence geopolitically replacing Western partners. Relations with France/EU
severely strained. ECOWAS sanctions partially lifted July 2022 after transition
timeline negotiation but diplomatic isolation from Western partners deepened.
Russia relationship warming while West-Mali relationship at historic low.

2023.assessment: Mali experienced significant diplomatic isolation in 2023. France withdrew all military forces by August 2023. MINUSMA mandate terminated. AES (Alliance of Sahel States) with Burkina Faso and Niger formed as alternative framework. EU suspended some cooperation programs. Relations with ECOWAS normalized but strained.

2024.assessment: Continued significant diplomatic isolation in 2024. AES framework consolidating as alternative to ECOWAS. MINUSMA withdrawal completed end 2023. Russian/Wagner security partnership deepened. Western diplomatic engagement reduced but not severed. China maintained economic ties.

sources.citation: US State Department, Mali bilateral relations 2024

2025.assessment: Mali expected to remain in partial diplomatic isolation in 2025. AES consolidating as geopolitical alternative. Transition election timeline unclear.

sources.citation: US State Department, Mali country information, 2025

2000.assessment: FDI inflows to Mali were approximately 2.8% of GDP in 2000. Gold mining was the dominant sector attracting foreign investment. Mali had several major gold mines (Syama, Morila, Loulo) operated by international mining companies. The stable democratic government provided a favorable investment climate.

2010.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.4% of GDP in 2010. Gold mining sector driving investment —
Mali is one of Africa's top gold producers. AngloGold Ashanti, Randgold, and
other mining companies operating. Stable ATT government providing investment
confidence. Cotton sector receiving some investment. Normal international
investment flows for a frontier market.

2011.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.7% of GDP in 2011. Pre-crisis peak. Gold prices at
historic highs in 2011 (>$1,900/oz) attracted gold exploration FDI.
Mining companies expanding operations. MNLA formed October 2011 but had
not yet disrupted operations. Stable investment climate in its final year.

2012.assessment:
FDI collapsed to approximately 0.9% of GDP in 2012. The coup and territorial
loss caused investment flight. Mining companies suspended exploration in northern
Mali. ECOWAS sanctions and international isolation froze new investment.
Existing gold operations in southern Mali continued providing minimal flows.
Worst FDI performance in the priority period.

2013.assessment:
FDI recovered to approximately 1.8% of GDP in 2013. Operation Serval restored
security in northern Mali. Elections and civilian government restored investor
confidence. Mining companies resumed operations. International aid resumption
created secondary investment climate. MINUSMA security presence reassured
investors in southern and central operations.

2014.assessment:
FDI recovered to approximately 2.0% of GDP in 2014. Civilian government
and IMF program on track provided investment confidence. Gold mining sector
performing. New mine expansions and exploration in southern Mali. Northern
Mali insecurity limiting northern exploration. Algiers Peace Process beginning.

2015.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2015. Recovery to moderate range. Algiers
Accord signed June 2015. Gold mining expansion continuing despite lower gold
prices. Telecom sector attracting investment. IMF ECF maintaining macro stability.
Ongoing insecurity in north not yet significantly deterring southern Mali investment.

2016.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.1% of GDP in 2016. Stable at moderate level. Gold mining
driving investment. Violence spreading from north to Mopti creating new security
risk for investors but not yet critically deterring national-level investment.
IMF ECF on track. Economy growing.

2017.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.5% of GDP in 2017. Gold prices recovering driving investment
in mining sector. Economy growing strongly. G5 Sahel Force providing additional
security signal for investors. New gold mine exploration and expansion. Telecom
and banking sectors attracting investment. Insecurity spreading but Bamako
economy continuing to attract capital.

2018.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.8% of GDP in 2018. Near-peak investment level. Gold mining
sector expanding with new mine developments. Keïta re-elected providing political
continuity signal. Despite Mopti security deterioration, Bamako-centered economy
maintained investor confidence. Telecoms and banking continued attracting investment.

2019.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.6% of GDP in 2019. Modestly below 2018 peak. Political
protests against Keïta government creating some investor caution. Security
situation worsening nationally. Gold sector still performing. M5-RFP protests
building uncertainty. Formal sector and gold investment maintained.

2020.assessment:
FDI fell sharply to approximately 1.4% of GDP in 2020. COVID-19 global FDI
decline affected Mali. August 2020 coup caused ECOWAS sanctions and political
uncertainty deterring new investment. International companies reducing
commitments. Gold sector maintained but new exploration investment frozen.
Political transition uncertainty deterring long-term investment decisions.

2021.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.5% of GDP in 2021. Marginal recovery from 2020. Second
coup May 2021 maintained international isolation. ECOWAS sanctions. IMF program
suspended. International investors remaining cautious. Gold sector continuing
to attract some investment given high gold prices. Wagner Group deployment
creating investor uncertainty about the security/geopolitical environment.

2022.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.7% of GDP in 2022. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January 2022
allowing partial recovery. Gold prices elevated. However, French expulsion,
Wagner presence, IMF program suspension, and continued insecurity maintained
investor caution. New investment primarily in existing gold operations. No
new large-scale exploration given security and political risk.

2023.assessment: FDI to Mali approximately 1.5% of GDP in 2023. Security instability under military junta and departure of French forces contributed to investor uncertainty. Gold mining continued but expansion slowed. Sanctions environment and geopolitical uncertainty dampened new investment.

2024.assessment: FDI to Mali approximately 1.2% of GDP in 2024. Security instability under military junta and departure of French forces contributed to investor uncertainty. Gold mining continued but expansion slowed. Sanctions environment and geopolitical uncertainty dampened new investment.

2025.assessment: FDI to Mali approximately 1.0% of GDP in 2025. Security instability under military junta and departure of French forces contributed to investor uncertainty. Gold mining continued but expansion slowed. Sanctions environment and geopolitical uncertainty dampened new investment.

2000.assessment: Mali maintained full or near-full sovereignty over its territory in 2000. No foreign military forces were present. The country was at peace and the government controlled all its territory.

sources.citation: US AFRICOM overview; French Ministry of Defense, Mali bilateral, 2000

sources.citation: US AFRICOM overview; French Ministry of Defense, Mali bilateral, 2009

2010.assessment:
Limited invited advisors in 2010. France maintained small military advisory
presence (under historical defense agreement). US had a small counter-terrorism
training presence (Operation Enduring Freedom - Trans Sahara). No significant
foreign combat forces. Mali retained full sovereignty over military operations.
AQIM presence in far north but no foreign CT operations in Mali.

2011.assessment:
Limited invited advisors in 2011. Same as 2010 — France and US small advisory
presence. Libya's collapse flooding northern Mali with weapons but no foreign
military intervention yet. MNLA formed October 2011. Pre-crisis baseline
with limited invited foreign military presence.

2012.assessment:
Contested/mixed presence in 2012. Following the March coup, most foreign
military advisors reduced or suspended cooperation. ECOWAS threatened military
intervention but negotiated a political solution instead. ECOWAS standby forces
on alert. France suspended bilateral military cooperation. No major foreign
combat operations yet — that came in January 2013 with Serval.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali foreign military 2012; ECOWAS intervention threat

2013.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2013. Operation Serval (French) launched January
11, 2013 — at Mali government request — with up to 4,000 French troops conducting
active combat operations against jihadist groups. MINUSMA (UN) deployed July 2013
with ~12,000 peacekeepers. ECOWAS forces (AFISMA) also present. EU training mission
(EUTM Mali) began. Multiple foreign military presences, all invited.

sources.citation: Operation Serval Jan 2013 (4,000 French); MINUSMA July 2013 (12,000 UN)

2014.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2014. Operation Barkhane formally launched August
1, 2014, replacing Serval — broader regional operation with ~3,000 French troops
covering Mali and G5 Sahel countries. MINUSMA maintaining ~12,000 peacekeepers
(became UN's deadliest mission). EUTM Mali training mission continuing. All
invited forces but substantial foreign military footprint.

2015.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2015. Barkhane (~3,000-4,000 French) conducting
active CT operations. MINUSMA (~12,000 UN peacekeepers) suffering casualties.
EUTM Mali training continuing. Algiers Accord signed June 2015 providing peace
framework. G5 Sahel developing regional security architecture. All forces
present at Mali government invitation.

sources.citation: Barkhane ~3-4,000 French; MINUSMA ~12,000; Algiers Accord June 2015

2016.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2016. Barkhane active across G5 Sahel region
with Mali as the primary base. MINUSMA (~12,000) suffering continued casualties
from jihadist attacks. EUTM training Malian forces. US special operations
forces (SOCAFRICA) supporting CT intelligence. All invited, but foreign
military presence now deeply integrated into security architecture.

sources.citation: Barkhane + MINUSMA 12,000 + EUTM + US SOF; all invited 2016

2017.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2017. G5 Sahel Joint Force formally launched
with French support (~5,000 troops from five countries). Barkhane continuing.
MINUSMA maintaining presence. EUTM Mali expanding training. US SOF maintaining
presence. All invited. Foreign military presence peak in this period; Mali
security increasingly dependent on external forces.

sources.citation: G5 Sahel JF launched; Barkhane; MINUSMA; EUTM; US SOF 2017

2018.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2018. Barkhane increasing operations as violence
spread. MINUSMA largest UN mission globally (~13,000 troops). EUTM Mali
continuing. G5 Sahel JF operational. US maintained SOF presence. All invited.
Keïta re-elected August 2018 committed to continued foreign military engagement
for security.

sources.citation: Barkhane; MINUSMA 13,000; EUTM; G5 JF; US SOF all invited 2018

2019.assessment:
Significant invited forces in 2019. Barkhane continuing operations with
increasing casualties. MINUSMA (~13,000) suffering heavy casualties. G5 Sahel
JF operating across region. EUTM training. US announced partial troop
withdrawal review from Sahel. All present at Mali government invitation.
Security showing no improvement despite heavy foreign presence.

sources.citation: Barkhane; MINUSMA 13,000; G5 JF; EUTM; US pullback review 2019

2020.assessment:
Contested mixed presence in 2020. Barkhane continuing but post-August 2020 coup
France suspended some cooperation with junta. MINUSMA continued (UN not
responding to coup by reducing mandate). EUTM Mali suspended training mission
temporarily. The coup created ambiguity about the consent basis for foreign
military presence. Wagner Group negotiations beginning post-coup.

sources.citation: Barkhane suspended some cooperation; MINUSMA continued; EUTM suspended; Wagner negotiations 2020

2021.assessment:
Contested mixed presence in 2021. Barkhane operations continued but relations
with junta deteriorating. France announced Barkhane restructuring (August 2021)
citing failure to cooperate. Wagner Group deployed to Mali — not invited by
a recognized civilian government. MINUSMA continuing but access restrictions
beginning. EU/NATO training missions under review. Complex competing foreign
military presences emerging.

sources.citation: Barkhane deteriorating; France Aug 2021 restructuring; Wagner deployed; MINUSMA access restrictions 2021

2022.assessment:
Contested mixed presence in 2022. France expelled February 2022 (Barkhane
operations in Mali ended). Wagner Group (1,000-2,000 personnel) operating
alongside FAMA — invited by junta but internationally controversial. MINUSMA
continuing with heavy access restrictions from junta. EUTM Mali suspended.
Denmark withdrew troops. Major shift from Western-led to Russia/Wagner security
architecture — a geopolitically contested transition.

sources.citation: France expelled Feb 2022; Barkhane ended; Wagner 1,000-2,000; MINUSMA restricted; EUTM suspended

2023.assessment: Significant foreign military presence in Mali in 2023 but transitioning. French Barkhane Force and Takuba Task Force completed withdrawal by August 2023. MINUSMA UN peacekeeping (around 15,000 troops) mandate terminated by junta in June 2023, completing withdrawal by end of year. Wagner Group (Russian) arrived to fill security role — invited by junta.

sources.citation: MINUSMA withdrawal report; French MoD Mali withdrawal, 2023

2024.assessment: Wagner Group (rebranded Africa Corps under Russian state control) maintained significant presence in Mali in 2024, estimated 1,000-2,000 personnel. Russian military advisors replaced Western forces. Wagner suffered casualties in fighting jihadist groups in northern Mali.

sources.citation: US AFRICOM report on Wagner/Africa Corps in Mali, 2024

2025.assessment: Russian Africa Corps expected to remain as dominant foreign military presence in Mali through 2025. Junta security partnership with Russia deepening.

2000.assessment: Mali experienced minimal refugee displacement in 2000. The country was at peace under democratic governance. Small numbers of Malian economic migrants went abroad but no refugee crisis existed. Mali hosted a small number of Mauritanian and other refugees from earlier conflicts.

2009.assessment: Mali experienced minimal refugee displacement in 2009. The country was at peace under democratic governance. Small numbers of Malian economic migrants went abroad but no refugee crisis existed. Mali hosted a small number of Mauritanian and other refugees from earlier conflicts.

2010.assessment:
Very low displacement in 2010. Mali was a relatively stable state with minor
security concerns in the far north (AQIM). Approximately 20,000-25,000 displaced
persons total (primarily residual from earlier Tuareg conflicts). No new mass
displacement events. Some voluntary returns from earlier displacements.

2011.assessment:
Low displacement in 2011. Slight increase from 2010 as MNLA formed (October
2011) and began early operations. Arms influx from Libya. Approximately 50,000
displaced total but limited new outflows. Tuareg communities in far north
showing displacement precursors. Libya's collapse created refugee return flows
of Malians from Libya adding to displacement count.

2012.assessment:
Mass displacement in 2012 — the peak crisis year. The MNLA/jihadist seizure
of northern Mali (January-April 2012) generated approximately 500,000-600,000
displaced persons — about 3% of the 17 million population. Approximately
200,000 fled to neighboring countries (Mauritania, Burkina Faso, Niger, Algeria)
as refugees; 300,000+ became IDPs in southern Mali. Worst displacement event
in Mali's modern history.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2012; IDMC data; 200,000 refugees + 300,000+ IDPs

2013.assessment:
Continued high displacement in 2013 but some returns beginning. Operation
Serval restored major northern cities. Some IDPs and refugees began returning.
Approximately 400,000-500,000 still displaced (IDPs + refugees) by end of 2013.
Returns started in H2 2013 following elections and MINUSMA deployment. New
displacement from ongoing conflict in remote areas partially offset by returns.

2014.assessment:
Moderate displacement continuing in 2014. Gradual returns of IDPs and refugees
from neighboring countries. Kidal crisis (May 2014) created new displacement.
Approximately 250,000-300,000 still displaced. UNHCR facilitating voluntary
repatriation. Continued insecurity in Kidal and Gao regions preventing full
return. New displacement from jihadist attacks smaller than 2012-2013 crisis.

2015.assessment:
Moderate displacement continuing in 2015. Algiers Accord signed June 2015 creating
framework for durable returns. Approximately 130,000-150,000 still displaced
(IDPs + refugees) down from 2012 crisis peak. New displacement from jihadist
attacks spreading to central Mali. Net displacement declining as returns
outpaced new flows in northern regions.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2015; Algiers Accord return framework

2016.assessment:
Moderate displacement in 2016. New displacement from violence spreading to
central Mali (Mopti region) increasing IDP numbers. Returns from north partially
offset by new displacement in center. Total displaced approximately 150,000-200,000.
Algiers Accord implementation stalling creating uncertainty for returnees.

2017.assessment:
Displacement rising again in 2017. JNIM formation March 2017 intensified attacks
in central Mali driving new IDP flows. Dogon-Fulani intercommunal violence in
Mopti creating displacement. Approximately 200,000-250,000 displaced. JNIM
attacks on villages displacing rural populations. Northern returns continuing
but offset by central Mali new displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2017; JNIM March 2017; Mopti intercommunal displacement

2018.assessment:
Significant displacement in 2018 as central Mali violence intensified.
Mopti region saw mass displacement from intercommunal and jihadist violence.
Approximately 400,000-500,000 displaced (IDPs) by year end. New displacement
dramatically exceeding returns. Kayes region beginning to see displacement.
JNIM and ISGS expanding their areas of operation and creating new displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2018; IDMC; Mopti mass displacement

2019.assessment:
Major displacement surge in 2019. Ogossagou massacre (March 2019, 160+ killed)
created mass displacement in Mopti region. Approximately 200,000-250,000 new
displacements in the year. Total stock of IDPs reached ~400,000-500,000.
Refugees in neighboring countries remaining at approximately 130,000-140,000.
UNHCR reported 60,000+ new IDP movements after Ogossagou alone.

2020.assessment:
Severe displacement in 2020. August 2020 coup created urban displacement in
Bamako. Total IDP stock reached approximately 400,000-500,000 by end of 2020.
Nationwide violence generating continuous new displacement. Refugees in
neighboring countries remaining elevated. Ogossagou II massacre (February 2020)
created new displacement spikes. COVID-19 prevented UNHCR returns facilitation.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2020; Ogossagou II Feb 2020; IDMC

2021.assessment:
Mass displacement in 2021. Total stock reached approximately 700,000+ IDPs
by end of 2021. Nationwide violence including second coup, JNIM, ISGS, and
Wagner Group operations creating displacement across all regions. Refugees
in neighboring countries approximately 150,000-160,000. Annual new displacement
estimated at 350,000-400,000 new IDPs. One of the worst displacement years.

sources.citation: UNHCR Mali 2021; IDMC; JNIM/ISGS/Wagner all generating displacement

2022.assessment:
Catastrophic displacement in 2022. Moura massacre (March 2022, 500+ killed)
created major displacement wave. Wagner operations throughout the country
generating displacement from civilian targeting. Total IDP stock reached
approximately 800,000-900,000 by end of 2022. Refugees in neighboring countries
approximately 170,000-180,000. Annual new displacement 400,000+. Second highest
displacement year of the period (behind overall cumulative stock growth).

2023.assessment: Mali experienced mass displacement in 2023. The continued jihadist insurgency in the north and center (JNIM, ISGS) displaced over 800,000 people internally. Approximately 170,000+ Malians were refugees in neighboring countries (Mauritania, Burkina Faso, Niger). MINUSMA withdrawal in 2023 increased insecurity.

2024.assessment: Continued mass displacement in 2024. Jihadist control over large areas of northern and central Mali increased IDP numbers. Total displaced (IDPs + refugees abroad) estimated at over 1 million. Wagner/Africa Corps operations did not significantly reduce insecurity. Humanitarian access severely restricted in conflict zones.

2025.assessment: Mass displacement expected to continue in 2025 absent significant security improvement.

2010.assessment:
No significant international sanctions on Mali in 2010. Mali was a recognized
democracy in good standing with ECOWAS, AU, and the international community.
Normal aid and trade relations maintained. IMF ECF program ongoing. No
sanctions of any kind in force.

2011.assessment:
No significant international sanctions on Mali in 2011. Pre-crisis normal
international standing. MNLA forming but no international response yet.
Full ECOWAS, AU, UN membership in good standing.

2012.assessment:
Significant sanctions in 2012 following March coup. ECOWAS imposed broad
economic sanctions including borders closure, asset freeze on junta leaders,
trade and financial embargoes. AU suspended Mali from all activities. US
suspended most aid. EU suspended budget support. Sanctions lasted from March
to approximately December 2012 when ECOWAS partially eased restrictions
following transitional framework negotiations.

sources.citation: ECOWAS sanctions on Mali post-coup March 2012

2013.assessment:
Sanctions substantially lifted in 2013. ECOWAS lifted most sanctions following
transitional framework and Operation Serval. Elections (July-August 2013) and
civilian government restored led to full sanctions removal. AU re-admitted Mali.
International aid resumed. UN MINUSMA deployed. Only targeted sanctions on
specific coup leaders/jihadist individuals remained from UN Security Council.

sources.citation: ECOWAS sanctions lifted 2013; UNSC targeted sanctions

2014.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2014. No broad trade or economic
sanctions. Mali under civilian government in good international standing.
UN Security Council Resolution 2100 (MINUSMA) maintained targeted sanctions
against jihadist individuals/groups. Normal international relations restored
with full AU, ECOWAS, IMF engagement.

sources.citation: UNSC targeted sanctions on jihadist individuals Mali 2014

2015.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2015. Same as prior year — UNSC targeted
sanctions on jihadist leaders and spoilers of the Algiers peace process. Algiers
Accord signed June 2015. Normal international relations. No sanctions on
Malian government entities.

2016.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2016. UNSC targeted sanctions on
jihadist individuals and Algiers spoilers. Civilian government in good
standing. No sanctions on Malian government. IMF ECF continuing normally.

2017.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2017. UNSC sanctions expanded to include
more jihadist individuals following JNIM formation March 2017. No sanctions
on Malian government. Civilian government in good standing with international
community. IMF ECF active.

sources.citation: UNSC targeted sanctions Mali 2017; JNIM individuals added

2018.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2018. UNSC targeted sanctions on jihadist
individuals. Disputed elections did not trigger international sanctions against
the government. Election irregularities condemned diplomatically but no
sanctions imposed. IMF program continuing.

2019.assessment:
Only targeted individual sanctions in 2019. UNSC targeted sanctions continued.
No new sanctions on government. Political tensions (M5-RFP protests) not
triggering international sanctions. IMF program continued. Normal international
aid and diplomatic relations.

2020.assessment:
Significant sanctions in 2020 following August coup. ECOWAS suspended Mali
and imposed targeted travel bans and asset freezes on coup leaders. AU suspended
Mali. US suspended military assistance. EU suspended budget support. Unlike 2012,
ECOWAS avoided broad economic sanctions affecting populations given humanitarian
concerns. Junta negotiated transition framework allowing partial relief.

sources.citation: ECOWAS targeted sanctions Mali post-August 2020 coup

2021.assessment:
Escalating sanctions in late 2021. ECOWAS maintained targeted sanctions from
August 2020 coup. May 2021 second coup triggered ECOWAS threat of broader
sanctions if transition timeline not met. By December 2021, ECOWAS rejected
junta's proposed 5-year transition timeline and threatened comprehensive
economic sanctions (implemented January 2022). IMF program suspended.

sources.citation: ECOWAS Mali sanctions escalation 2021; IMF program suspension

2022.assessment:
Comprehensive ECOWAS sanctions January-July 2022, then partially lifted.
ECOWAS imposed comprehensive economic sanctions January 9, 2022 (borders closed,
financial transactions suspended). Lifted July 2022 following negotiated 24-month
transition timeline. UNSC targeted sanctions continued. Western countries maintained
bilateral sanctions on specific junta individuals. IMF remained suspended. UN
Security Council arms embargo discussions ongoing.

sources.citation: ECOWAS comprehensive sanctions Jan 2022; lifted July 2022

2023.assessment: Mali faced comprehensive ECOWAS sanctions in 2023. ECOWAS imposed severe sanctions in January 2022 following the junta's refusal to commit to a transition timeline, then lifted major trade sanctions in July 2022 after a new transition agreement. By 2023, targeted sanctions on junta leaders remained but broad trade sanctions were lifted. France completed military withdrawal. Wagner Group presence confirmed.

2024.assessment: Mali under military junta in 2024 with targeted individual sanctions from ECOWAS on junta leaders. MINUSMA UN peacekeeping mission withdrew (completed by end 2023). AES (Alliance of Sahel States) with Burkina Faso and Niger formed as alternative regional framework. Western sanctions remained on transition process violations.

2025.assessment: Targeted sanctions on Mali junta leaders expected to continue through 2025. Junta scheduled transition elections but credibility uncertain. AES framework replacing ECOWAS alignment. Continued Wagner/Russian security partnership.

Press freedom was relatively robust, assembly rights were generally respected. Some restrictions on civil society in northern regions and occasional police overreach.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World, Mali 2000
