2010.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 2 (1-7 scale, lower = more free) in 2010. Mali
was considered Partly Free with significant civil liberties. Freedom of
expression, assembly, and religion generally respected. Independent press
operating. Opposition parties active. Civil society vibrant. ATT government
respected constitutional constraints. One of Africa's more respected democracies.

2011.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 2 (unchanged from 2010) in 2011. Continued relatively
strong civil liberties. Press freedom maintained. Political opposition active.
Growing security concerns in north not yet significantly impacting civil liberties
in the south. MNLA formed October 2011 but pre-conflict status maintained.

2012.assessment:
Freedom House CL score sharply deteriorated to 5 in 2012. Military coup
suspended constitution and political rights. Military junta suspended
opposition activities. Independent media harassed. Northern Mali under
jihadist control imposed Sharia restricting freedoms. ECOWAS sanctions.
Worst civil liberties year in the priority period. Population in north
subject to jihadist repression.

2013.assessment:
Freedom House CL score improved to 4 in 2013. Operation Serval freed
northern Mali from jihadist Sharia. Elections held, restoring civilian
government. Press freedom partially restored. However, security situation
prevented full normalization. Conflict-related restrictions in northern
regions continued. Significant improvements from 2012 coup baseline.

2014.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 maintained in 2014. Civil liberties partially
restored under Keïta government. Press freedom recovered in Bamako.
Opposition parties active. However, northern regions remained conflict
zones with restricted freedoms. Security forces impunity documented.
Incomplete civil liberties normalization.

2015.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 maintained in 2015. Civil liberties stable.
Algiers Accord signed June 2015. Radisson Blu attack November 2015
leading to enhanced security measures. Media freedom maintained in Bamako
though self-censorship on security topics growing. Central Mali jihadist
activity beginning to restrict civil liberties in affected areas.

2016.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 in 2016. Civil liberties in Bamako relatively
maintained but increasingly restricted in conflict zones. State of
emergency in effect (declared in November 2015 following Radisson attack,
extended multiple times). Emergency powers allowing security forces expanded
detention and search authorities. Mopti conflict restricting freedoms in that region.

2017.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 in 2017. Civil liberties maintained in Bamako
but deteriorating across the conflict zone (expanding to cover central Mali).
State of emergency continued. Journalists self-censoring on security topics.
Growing areas where neither state nor civil society effectively operated.
JNIM formation March 2017 restricting freedom in areas under its influence.

2018.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 maintained in 2018. Disputed elections created
political tensions. Media reported on protests but faced increased government
pressure. State of emergency provisions restricting assembly in conflict areas.
Central Mali conflict zones effectively without civil liberty protections.
Urban Bamako maintained partial freedoms.

2019.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 in 2019. Civil liberties under significant stress.
M5-RFP protest movement demonstrating right of assembly still exercised in
Bamako. Growing impunity for security forces documented by Human Rights Watch
and Amnesty International. Conflict areas (Mopti, Gao, Kidal) effectively
without civil liberty protections. Security forces involved in civilian killings.

2020.assessment:
Freedom House CL score deteriorated to 5 in 2020. August coup removed elected
government; junta restricted assembly and political activity. M5-RFP protests
initially allowed but political parties' activities constrained. Conflict
zones (half the country) without civil liberty protections. COVID-19 lockdowns
added restrictions. Journalists faced increased harassment under transitional
authorities.

2021.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 5 maintained in 2021. Second coup maintained restrictions.
Protests banned by junta. Media independence sharply curtailed. Radio France
Internationale (RFI) suspended. Internet disruptions during political events.
Junta arrested political opponents. Conflict zones continued without civil
liberty protections. Wagner operations implicated in civilian atrocities.

2022.assessment:
Freedom House CL score deteriorated to 6 in 2022. Junta expanded media
restrictions — French TV channel TV5 Monde suspended (March 2022), RFI
suspension extended. Protests heavily restricted. Political party activities
banned. International NGOs expelled. Journalist arrests documented. Moura
massacre (March 2022) demonstrated civilian population fully without
protection from state/Wagner forces. Country approaching "Not Free" status.

2023.assessment: Civil liberties severely restricted in Mali in 2023 under military junta. Freedom House rated Mali as Not Free. Press freedom dramatically reduced, assembly restrictions, political activity banned, opposition leaders arrested.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World, Mali 2023

2024.assessment: Civil liberties severely restricted in Mali in 2024 under military junta. Freedom House rated Mali as Not Free. Press freedom dramatically reduced, assembly restrictions, political activity banned, opposition leaders arrested.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World, Mali 2024

2025.assessment: Civil liberties severely restricted in Mali in 2025 under military junta. Freedom House rated Mali as Not Free. Press freedom dramatically reduced, assembly restrictions, political activity banned, opposition leaders arrested.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World, Mali 2025

2000.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2000 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. President Konaré maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2001.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2001 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. President Konaré maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2002.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2002 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. President Konaré maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2003.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2003 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2004.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2004 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2005.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2005 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2006.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2006 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2007.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2007 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2008.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2008 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2009.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2009 was characterized by democratic competition with underlying stability. ATT's cross-party approach maintained broad elite coalition support. Inter-elite competition channeled through electoral processes. Military remained apolitical in this period.

2010.assessment:
Minor tensions but functional governance in 2010. ATT was in his second term
(2007-2012) and had built a broad governing coalition. Major political parties
(ADEMA, URD, CNID) cooperated in parliament. Military factions contained.
ATT governed through patronage and consensus-building. No significant elite
fractures threatening governance. Pre-crisis stability maintained.

2011.assessment:
Significant competition beginning to emerge in 2011. ATT's governing coalition
showing strain. Military officers frustrated with northern security management
(MNLA gaining strength). Junior officers increasingly critical of ATT's
perceived indifference to northern Mali. Coup conspiracies forming in the
military. Elite fragmentation visible with electoral politics heating up for
2012 elections. ATT constitutionally unable to run again.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2011; coup trajectory analysis

2012.assessment:
Complete fragmentation in 2012. March 2012 coup by Captain Amadou Sanogo
(CNRDRE junta) overthrew ATT. Internal junta divisions immediately emerged —
Sanogo vs senior military officers vs political class. ECOWAS mediation imposed
transition framework with civilian Dioncounda Traoré as interim president while
Sanogo retained real power. Multiple power centers competing. The worst elite
fragmentation in Mali's post-independence history.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali coup dynamics 2012; Sanogo/CNRDRE analysis

2013.assessment:
Active power struggles in 2013, gradually resolving. Elections July-August
restored civilian rule (Keïta elected). Sanogo arrested May 2013 after
alleged complicity in killings of soldiers. Legislative elections December
2013. Civilian elite gradually reasserting control. Military still influential
but visible leadership struggle between civilian and military factions. Keïta
building governing coalition.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali 2013; Sanogo arrested May 2013

2014.assessment:
Significant competition managed in 2014. Keïta government functioning but
facing opposition from multiple political parties and civil society. Sanogo
trial process creating political tensions (charges of complicity in killings).
Military influence continuing but civilian government nominally in control.
Coalition partners competing for patronage positions. Fragile but functioning
elite bargain under civilian rule.

2015.assessment:
Significant competition managed in 2015. Algiers Accord signed June 2015.
Keïta government coalition facing opposition. Growing tensions between government
and armed groups (CMA/MNLA) over Kidal. Military frustrated at security
failures. Civil society vocal. Elite consensus fragile but coup risk not
immediate. Security deterioration adding pressure.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2015; Algiers Accord

2016.assessment:
Significant competition managed in 2016. Keïta coalition maintained but
with growing patronage competition. Military officers increasingly critical
of government response to jihadist expansion. Opposition parties coalescing
against IBK. Corruption scandals (military equipment procurement fraud)
reducing elite legitimacy. Coup risk remaining but contained.

2017.assessment:
Significant competition managed in 2017. 2018 election campaigns beginning.
Multiple presidential candidates emerging within the political elite. Military
increasingly autonomous. IBK's son Karim Keïta's role in parliament generating
dynastic concerns. Civil society organizations (including M5-RFP precursors)
organizing. No immediate coup threat but coup risk trajectory increasing.

2018.assessment:
Open factional conflict in 2018. Disputed elections (August 2018) created deep
elite divisions. Opposition unified around Soumaïla Cissé contested Keïta's
re-election vigorously. Constitutional court controversy over election results.
Protests by opposition and civil society. Military frustration at civilian
governance growing. Elite consensus visibly fracturing around contested election.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2018; disputed elections Aug 2018

2019.assessment:
Open factional conflict in 2019. M5-RFP (Mouvement du 5 Juin - Rassemblement
des Forces Patriotiques) formed, unifying opposition parties, civil society, and
religious leaders (imam Mahmoud Dicko) against Keïta. Legislature suspended
following disputed legislative election results. Military officers increasingly
restless. Calls for Keïta's resignation openly voiced. Coup trajectory accelerating.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2019; M5-RFP formation

2020.assessment:
Complete fragmentation in 2020. August 18, 2020 coup by Colonel Assimi Goïta
and other military officers ousted Keïta. Keïta resigned under military pressure.
Junta formed National Committee for the Salvation of the People (CNSP). ECOWAS
mediation produced transitional framework with civilian president Bah Ndaw but
Goïta retained real power. Deep split between civilian political class and
military junta. Second-tier military officers competing within junta.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2020; Goïta coup Aug 2020

2021.assessment:
Active power struggles in 2021. Second coup May 24, 2021 resolved intra-elite
competition with Goïta removing Bah Ndaw and Moctar Ouane, establishing full
junta control. Goïta appointed transitional president. Within the junta, Goïta
consolidated power but competition among coup participants (Sidibé, Camara etc.)
continued. Political parties and civil society suppressed, removing alternative
elite channels. Wagner negotiations conducted by narrow junta circle.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2021; second coup May 2021

2022.assessment:
Significant competition managed within junta in 2022. Goïta maintaining power
but with competition from other junta members. Wagner Group presence creating
parallel power structure and loyalty competition. Political parties banned reducing
civilian elite channels. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January 2022 but international
isolation continued. Within the narrow junta elite, Goïta dominant but
security apparatus competition present.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali elite dynamics 2022; Goïta + Wagner parallel

2023.assessment: Elite cohesion in Mali in 2023 was characterized by junta unity with managed tensions. The Transition Charter government under Goïta maintained military cohesion despite some intra-junta tensions. Civilian technocrats served as prime ministers under military oversight. Previous civilian political elite marginalized.

2024.assessment: Junta continued to maintain elite cohesion in 2024. Military leadership unified around Goïta. Some tensions between pro-Wagner and other military factions but contained. Transition timeline disputes managed internally.

2025.assessment: Junta elite cohesion expected to continue in 2025, possibly under increasing strain as transition timeline pressures mount.

2010.assessment:
Institutions functioning but flawed in 2010. Mali was a multi-party democracy
under President Touré (ATT). Legislature (Assemblée Nationale) operational.
Judiciary nominally independent but weak capacity. Elections held on schedule.
Civil service functioning. However, northern Mali had traditionally weak state
presence and chronic governance deficits. Overall considered a relatively
successful African democracy compared to regional peers.

2011.assessment:
Institutions functional but showing strain in 2011. ATT government governing
without a majority coalition, dependent on patronage networks. MNLA formed
October 2011. Northern governance weakening. Legislature and judiciary
nominally functional. Growing corruption allegations undermining ATT's
legitimacy. Still considered functioning democracy prior to 2012 collapse.

2012.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2012. Military coup March 22, 2012 suspended
the constitution, dissolved the government, and established the CNRDRE military
junta. Constitutional court and parliament suspended. Elections cancelled. The
junta negotiated a transition framework with ECOWAS that allowed a nominal
transitional government, but real power remained with coup leaders.
Worst institutional breakdown in Mali since independence.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2012; CNRDRE coup March 2012

2013.assessment:
Institutions partially recovering in 2013. Elections held July-August 2013;
Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta (IBK) elected with 77% in runoff. Civilian government
restored. Legislature reconstituted (December 2013 legislative elections).
Constitutional court restored. However, northern regions still outside state
authority. Institutions weak and recovering from 2012 collapse. Security
sector reform needed but not advancing.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2013; IBK elected Aug 2013

2014.assessment:
Institutions partially functional in 2014. Civilian government functioning but
weak. Legislature active but IBK governing with narrow support coalition. Judiciary
showing continued capacity deficits. Kidal crisis (May 2014) demonstrated limits
of government authority. Security sector still weak. IMF program supporting
fiscal management. Civil society active in Bamako.

2015.assessment:
Institutions partially functional in 2015. Algiers Accord signed June 2015
providing framework for peace and reconciliation. Government functioning but
with significant capacity gaps. Legislature and judiciary operational in Bamako.
Northern regions still without state institutions. Rising insecurity reducing
state reach in central Mali.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2015; Algiers Accord June 2015

2016.assessment:
Institutions partially functional in 2016. Government operating but with
growing corruption and patronage. IBK's RPM party lacking strong majority.
Constitutional reforms promised but not implemented. Security sector reform
stalled. Decentralization incomplete. Central Mali state presence declining.
IMF ECF on track maintaining some fiscal discipline.

2017.assessment:
Institutions partially functional in 2017. Government operating in Bamako.
Local governments in central and northern Mali increasingly unable to function
as jihadist violence expanded. Significant portions of the country without
state institutions. Legislature active but opposition parties frustrated with
IBK governance. Elections scheduled for 2018.

2018.assessment:
Institutions partially functional in 2018. Keïta re-elected August 2018 amid
widespread fraud allegations. Opposition disputed results. Constitutional court
controversy. Legislature reconstituted after disputed elections. Security sector
unable to halt jihadist expansion. Civil society increasingly vocal but elections
marred by irregularities. Governance legitimacy declining.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2018; disputed Aug 2018 elections

2019.assessment:
Institutions functioning but severely strained in 2019. IBK government facing
mounting legitimacy crisis. Constitutional court controversy (legislative elections).
Legislature suspended following disputed election results. Civil society protests
(Mouvement du 5 Juin building). Security sector demonstrably failing to protect
citizens. Calls for resignation mounting. Only Bamako core institutions functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2019; M5-RFP protests building

2020.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2020. August 2020 coup (Colonel Goïta)
overthrew President Keïta. Constitution suspended. Legislature dissolved.
Transitional government formed under civilian Bah Ndaw as president but
Goïta held real power. ECOWAS negotiated 18-month transition framework.
Only military junta and transitional executive operational; other institutions
nominally functioning or suspended.

2021.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2021. Second coup May 24, 2021 — Goïta
removed transitional President Bah Ndaw and PM Moctar Ouane. Goïta appointed
himself transitional president. Completely military-controlled governance.
Legislature (Conseil National de Transition) as appointed advisory body only.
Elections postponed indefinitely. ECOWAS sanctions. IMF program suspended.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2021; second coup May 2021; Goïta self-appointed transitional president

2022.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2022. Junta rule with Goïta as transitional
president. Transition charter modified to allow 24-month transition with
elections promised for 2024 (later postponed further). Freedom of assembly
restricted; protests banned. Wagner forces operating alongside FAMA. MINUSMA
facing access restrictions. Media censorship increased. Rule of law deteriorating.

sources.citation: Freedom House Mali 2022; junta rule; Wagner presence

2023.assessment: Mali's institutions were severely degraded under military junta in 2023. Civilian parliament suspended. Constitutional court replaced with junta-appointed bodies. Judiciary under executive influence. Transition charter replacing constitution. Elections postponed. No meaningful democratic participation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World Mali 2023

2024.assessment: Institutions remained severely degraded in 2024. Junta proposed new constitution referendum. Political parties banned from electoral activities. Civil society organizations restricted. Junta announced elections but timeline unclear.

2025.assessment: Expected continued severe institutional degradation under junta through 2025.

2000.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2000 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2001.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2001 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2002.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2002 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2003.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2003 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2004.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2004 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2005.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2005 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2006.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2006 was low. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare. 2006 Tuareg rebellion introduced more conflict events in northern Mali.

2007.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2007 was low. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare. 2006 Tuareg rebellion introduced more conflict events in northern Mali.

2008.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2008 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2009.assessment: Political violence in Mali in 2009 was minimal. Under democratic governance, large-scale political violence was rare.

2010.assessment:
Approximately 60 conflict-related deaths in 2010. Very low level of political
violence. Previous Tuareg rebellions had been suppressed. AQIM presence in
the far north caused sporadic incidents (kidnappings, attacks on security forces)
but national violence level was minimal. ATT government maintained relative
peace throughout the country.

2011.assessment:
Approximately 75 conflict-related deaths in 2011. Slight increase from 2010.
AQIM kidnappings and attacks in the north increasing. MNLA formed October 2011
but no significant violence yet. Arms influx from Libya's collapse fueling
AQIM and proto-MNLA formations. Still pre-crisis, violence at minimal level.

2012.assessment:
Approximately 1,800 conflict-related deaths in 2012 — massive spike. The MNLA
rebellion began January 2012; by April MNLA, Ansar Dine, MUJAO, and AQIM
jointly controlled all of northern Mali. Military coup March 2012. Significant
combat between FAMA and rebel/jihadist forces across northern Mali. Civilian
massacres and atrocities documented in northern towns. Worst year for violence
in the priority period.

2013.assessment:
Approximately 1,200 conflict-related deaths in 2013. Still elevated from the
crisis peak. Operation Serval (January 2013) drove jihadists from major
cities but guerrilla activity continued. French and FAMA combat operations
in northern Mali. MINUSMA deployed July 2013. Kidal remained contested.
Despite elections and civilian government restoration, conflict continued.

2014.assessment:
Approximately 800 conflict-related deaths in 2014. Violence declining from
2012-2013 crisis peak but still at elevated level. Jihadist activity continuing
in northern Mali. Kidal crisis (May 2014) when government attempted to enter
Kidal. Algiers Peace Process beginning. MINUSMA and Barkhane operations active.
Barkhane formally replacing Serval August 2014.

2015.assessment:
Approximately 700 conflict-related deaths in 2015. Violence continuing at
elevated but declining levels from the 2012 peak. Algiers Accord signed
June 2015. Jihadist attacks including on the Radisson Blu hotel Bamako
(November 2015, 20+ killed) showing jihadist groups reaching the capital.
Central Mali violence beginning. MINUSMA and Barkhane active.

2016.assessment:
Approximately 850 conflict-related deaths in 2016. Violence increasing again
as insurgency spread to central Mali. Mopti region seeing first significant
attacks. Northern Mali jihadist groups attacking MINUSMA peacekeepers regularly
(MINUSMA became the most deadly UN mission globally). Algiers Accord implementation
stalling. Intercommunal tensions emerging in central Mali.

2017.assessment:
Approximately 1,100 conflict-related deaths in 2017. Significant increase as
JNIM formed March 2017, unifying jihadist groups. Central Mali (Mopti) saw
dramatic increase in violence including attacks on Dogon and Fulani communities.
Bamako saw attacks on embassies and security forces. G5 Sahel Force established
but not yet operational. MINUSMA suffering increased casualties.

2018.assessment:
Approximately 1,500 conflict-related deaths in 2018. Violence continuing to
escalate. Mopti region became the center of worst intercommunal violence with
jihadist groups deliberately fomenting Dogon-Fulani conflict. Mass civilian
killings increasingly common. Keïta re-elected August 2018 amid security crisis.
ISGS (Islamic State in Greater Sahara) also active in Ménaka and Gao regions.

2019.assessment:
Approximately 2,800 conflict-related deaths in 2019 — highest since 2012.
Ogossagou massacre March 2019 (160+ Fulani killed by Dogon hunters) was the
worst single incident. Violence spreading to Kayes region in western Mali
(previously peaceful). JNIM and ISGS both highly active. MINUSMA now operating
across nearly the whole country. Civilian casualty rate historically high.

2020.assessment:
Approximately 3,200 conflict-related deaths in 2020 — exceeded the 2012 crisis
year. Violence now a nationwide crisis. Ogossagou II massacre February 2020
(50+ killed). August 2020 coup created additional political violence. JNIM and
ISGS both intensifying operations. FAMAs ability to respond degraded by coup
and political uncertainty. Malian state authority nearly collapsed in rural areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali 2020; UCDP data; Ogossagou II Feb 2020

2021.assessment:
Approximately 3,800 conflict-related deaths in 2021. Second highest year in
Mali's conflict history. Second coup May 2021 further degraded state response.
JNIM and ISGS operations intensified. Wagner Group began deploying, with
operations involving civilian massacres. Sahel-wide violence spillover affecting
border areas. No effective counterinsurgency strategy. Widespread civilian targeting.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali 2021; UCDP data; Wagner deployment

2022.assessment:
Approximately 5,400 conflict-related deaths in 2022 — highest in Mali's modern
history. Moura massacre March 2022 (Wagner/FAMA killed 500+ civilians over 4
days). Barkhane expelled February 2022. JNIM and ISGS continued intensifying.
Wagner Group conducted numerous civilian massacre operations. UN MINUSMA facing
access restrictions from junta. Nationwide counterinsurgency offensive with
massive civilian cost.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali 2022; Moura massacre March 2022; UCDP data

2023.assessment: Extremely high political violence in Mali in 2023. Jihadist attacks (JNIM, ISGS) throughout center and north. Wagner/Africa Corps operations with civilian casualties. Inter-communal violence. ACLED recorded approximately 3800 events.

2024.assessment: Extremely high political violence in Mali in 2024. Jihadist attacks (JNIM, ISGS) throughout center and north. Wagner/Africa Corps operations with civilian casualties. Inter-communal violence. ACLED recorded approximately 4500 events.

2025.assessment: Extremely high political violence in Mali in 2025. Jihadist attacks (JNIM, ISGS) throughout center and north. Wagner/Africa Corps operations with civilian casualties. Inter-communal violence. ACLED recorded approximately 4800 events.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report Mali 2006

2007.assessment: The Malian government exercised effective but challenged territorial control in 2007. The democratic government of ATT maintained stability throughout most of Mali's territory. A brief Tuareg rebellion in northern Mali (Kidal/Menaka) began in 2006 but was limited in scope.

2009.assessment: The Malian government exercised full territorial control in 2009. The democratic government of ATT maintained stability throughout most of Mali's territory.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report Mali 2009

2010.assessment:
Near-complete territorial control in 2010. Government controlled virtually all
of Mali's territory including the north. The previous Tuareg rebellions (2006-2009)
had been suppressed or resolved through negotiations. ATT government maintained
effective governance throughout the country. Minor banditry in the north but no
organized territorial challenge to state authority.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2010; Uppsala Conflict Data Program

2011.assessment:
Near-full territorial control in 2011. The MNLA (Mouvement National de Libération
de l'Azawad) formed October 2011 but had not yet launched its rebellion. AQIM
presence in the far north was a security concern but government maintained nominal
control. Libya's collapse in 2011 allowed weapons and fighters to flow into Mali's
north, setting the stage for 2012 rebellion.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2011; MNLA formation October 2011

2012.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 38% of territory in 2012 — the worst year.
The MNLA rebellion began January 2012; by April 2012 the MNLA (and then jihadist
groups including Ansar Dine, MUJAO, AQIM) controlled all of northern Mali —
roughly 60-65% of national territory (Kidal, Gao, Timbuktu regions). The March
2012 coup further weakened central government capacity. The junta held the south
(Bamako, Mopti, Segou, Sikasso, Kayes) but with degraded institutional functioning.

2013.assessment:
Government control recovered substantially in 2013, to approximately 72% of
territory. Operation Serval (January 2013) drove jihadist groups out of major
northern cities (Kidal, Gao, Timbuktu). MNLA initially cooperated but then
contested Kidal. Some northern areas remained outside firm government control.
MINUSMA deployed July 2013 supporting stability. Keïta elected, civilian rule
restored. Significant areas still contested in Kidal region.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2013; Operation Serval assessment

2014.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 70% of territory in 2014. Northern regions
(particularly Kidal) remained contested — MNLA controlled Kidal city against
government forces, with the government prevented from entering in May 2014 (Kidal
crisis). MINUSMA and Barkhane maintaining fragile stability in northern cities.
Central Mali (Mopti) experiencing increasing jihadist activity.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2014; Kidal crisis May 2014

2015.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 68% of territory in 2015. Algiers Accord
signed June 2015 but implementation began slowly. Northern regions remained
significantly contested with armed groups active in Kidal, Gao, and Timbuktu.
MINUSMA and Barkhane providing security umbrella in north. Jihadist attacks
spreading into central Mali (Mopti region) for the first time.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2015; Algiers Accord June 2015

2016.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 65% of territory in 2016. Violence spreading
from north to central Mali (Mopti, Segou regions). Former north-only insurgency
becoming a nationwide insurgency. Northern regions (Kidal especially) remained
outside effective government control. MINUSMA and Barkhane maintaining presence
but unable to restore government authority in Kidal.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2016; Mopti region violence spreading

2017.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 60% of territory in 2017. Central Mali
(Mopti region) increasingly contested with jihadist and intercommunal violence.
JNIM (Jamaat Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin) formed March 2017 unifying jihadist
groups expanding their reach. G5 Sahel Force established but not yet operational.
Kidal still outside effective government control.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2017; JNIM formation March 2017

2018.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 55% of territory in 2018. Mopti region
became the epicenter of violence with jihadist attacks and intercommunal conflict
between Dogon and Fulani communities. Significant portions of Mopti and Gao
regions outside effective government control. Kidal firmly outside government
reach. Keïta re-elected August 2018 amid worsening security.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2018; Mopti violence epicenter

2019.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 50% of territory in 2019. The security
situation reached a critical point with violence spreading across three of the
five administrative regions. Mopti region saw mass civilian massacres (Ogossagou
massacre March 2019, 160+ killed). JNIM and ISGS expanding presence. Protests
against government for security failure building. Kidal still outside control.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2019; Ogossagou massacre March 2019

2020.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 42% of territory in 2020. Security
catastrophically deteriorating. August 2020 coup removed elected government
and created political uncertainty. JNIM and ISGS continued expanding influence
in northern and central regions. COVID-19 further strained state capacity.
Significant portions of Mopti, Gao, Kidal, Ménaka, and Taoudeni regions
effectively outside central government authority.

2021.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 38% of territory in 2021. Security continued
to deteriorate. May 2021 second coup maintained political dysfunction. Wagner
Group beginning to deploy as Barkhane withdrawal negotiated. Large swathes of
Mopti, Kidal, Gao, Ménaka, Taoudeni regions outside effective government authority.
State presence reduced to urban centers and main transport corridors.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2021; second coup May 2021; Wagner deployment

2022.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 35% of territory in 2022. Wagner forces
with FAMA conducting counterterrorism operations (Moura massacre March 2022,
500+ killed) but failing to restore meaningful territorial control. French
Barkhane expelled February 2022. JNIM and ISGS continued to control extensive
rural and semi-urban areas. ECOWAS sanctions lifted January 2022 but security
continued to deteriorate. State present only in major towns.

sources.citation: ACLED Mali data 2022; Moura massacre March 2022; Barkhane expulsion Feb 2022

2023.assessment: Mali government had very limited territorial control in 2023. Jihadist groups JNIM and ISGS controlled large portions of northern and central Mali. MINUSMA withdrawal accelerated territorial losses. Azawad (northern) area largely outside government control. Only Bamako and major southern towns firmly under government control.

sources.citation: UN Security Council report on Mali 2023

2024.assessment: Continued large-scale loss of territorial control in 2024. JNIM advance in center continued. CMA (Coordination of Azawad Movements) and GATIA fighting junta forces in north. Wagner/Africa Corps suffered significant casualties in northern Mali battles.

2025.assessment: Large portions of Mali territory expected to remain outside government control in 2025.

2010.assessment:
OBS score 22 in 2010 (survey year). Mali provided minimal budget information
to citizens. Budget documents not publicly accessible in full. The executive
budget proposal was the primary document produced but with limited detail and
accessibility. In-year reports and mid-year reviews minimal. Citizens and
legislators had very limited ability to scrutinize government spending.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey IBP Mali 2010: score 22

2011.assessment:
OBS score approximately 20 in 2011 (non-survey year; interpolated from 2010 score
of 22). Slight deterioration anticipated as MNLA rebellion developing in Q4 2011
may have distracted budget documentation. Still in the minimal range. ATT government
producing basic budget documents but accessibility remained limited.

sources.citation: OBS Mali interpolated from 2010 score 22; non-survey year

2012.assessment:
OBS score dropped to 11 in 2012 (survey year). The coup (March 2012) and
loss of northern territories severely disrupted budget processes. Junta governance
provided minimal financial transparency. Emergency spending not documented
publicly. Budget documents not produced on normal schedule. IMF program suspended.
One of the worst transparency years in the period.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey IBP Mali 2012: score 11

2013.assessment:
OBS score approximately 12 in 2013 (non-survey year; interpolated between
2012 score of 11 and 2015 score of 17). Slight improvement as civilian
government under Keïta restored some budget processes. Aid flows resuming
and international pressure for fiscal transparency. Still very low — institutions
rebuilding from coup shock.

sources.citation: OBS Mali interpolated from 2012:11 and 2015:17; non-survey

2014.assessment:
OBS score approximately 14 in 2014 (non-survey year; interpolated). Gradual
improvement as civilian government restored budget processes. IMF ECF program
on track requiring fiscal documentation. International donors demanding
greater transparency as condition for aid. Still in the scant range.

2015.assessment:
OBS score 17 in 2015 (survey year). Some improvement from 2012 trough.
Civilian government under Keïta producing more budget documents. Algiers
Accord creating some institutional pressure. IMF ECF maintaining financial
discipline requirements. Still in scant range — very limited public access
to budget information. Budget documents published but with limited detail
and poor accessibility.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey IBP Mali 2015: score 17

2016.assessment:
OBS score approximately 17 in 2016 (non-survey year; same as 2015 survey
score — stable). Government maintaining same budget transparency level.
IMF ECF on track. No significant change in budget documentation practices.

sources.citation: OBS Mali interpolated 2016; estimated stable at 2015 level

2017.assessment:
OBS score 17 in 2017 (survey year). Unchanged from 2015 score. Stagnant
budget transparency. Government not advancing disclosure practices. IMF ECF
maintaining some discipline. G5 Sahel security spending creating new
off-budget pressures. Elections in 2018 not generating transparency reforms.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey IBP Mali 2017: score 17

2018.assessment:
OBS score approximately 20 in 2018 (non-survey year; interpolated between

IMF ECF and international donors creating incremental transparency pressure.
Election year may have influenced some budget disclosure for credibility.

2019.assessment:
OBS score 23 in 2019 (survey year). Modest improvement — highest score
of the priority period. Some budget disclosure improvements under Keïta
government. Mid-term budget reviews published. Citizens budget produced
(in limited form). Despite political protests, government publishing more
budget information. Still in minimal transparency range.

sources.citation: Open Budget Survey IBP Mali 2019: score 23

OBS score approximately 22 in 2020 (non-survey year; interpolated). August

outside normal budget framework. Budget transparency deteriorating in H2

2003.assessment: Mali was not yet an EITI member in 2003. The gold mining sector dominated extractive revenues. No systematic disclosure of mining revenues.

2004.assessment: Mali was an EITI candidate 2004. The gold mining sector dominated extractive revenues. Working toward disclosure standards.

2006.assessment: Mali was an EITI candidate 2006. The gold mining sector dominated extractive revenues. Working toward disclosure standards.

2008.assessment: Mali was an EITI candidate 2008. The gold mining sector dominated extractive revenues. Working toward disclosure standards.

2009.assessment: Mali achieved EITI compliance around 2009-2010. 2009. The gold mining sector dominated extractive revenues. EITI reports published with corrective action requirements.

2010.assessment:
Mali was an EITI Candidate country in 2010 (candidate status since 2006,
working toward full compliance). Gold mining is Mali's dominant extractive
sector — production from major mines (Syama, Morila, Sadiola, Loulo). EITI
reports being produced covering gold revenues. Multi-stakeholder group (MSG)
active. Candidate status meant reports being produced but full compliance
validation not yet achieved. Active progress toward compliance.

sources.citation: EITI Mali 2010 candidate status; working toward compliance; gold mining sector

2011.assessment:
Mali achieved EITI Compliant status in 2011. This was a significant transparency
achievement for Mali's gold mining sector. EITI reports covering gold revenues
from major mines fully produced and reconciled with government receipts. Multi-
stakeholder group functioning. Revenue flows from AngloGold Ashanti (Sadiola,
Morila), Randgold (Loulo, Gounkoto), and other operators reconciled with
Ministry of Mines receipts. Meaningful transparency in extractive sector.

sources.citation: EITI Mali achieved Compliant status 2011; gold revenue reconciliation

2012.assessment:
Mali suspended from EITI in 2012 following the March coup. EITI rules require
that implementing countries have democratic/civilian governance — the CNRDRE
junta did not meet this requirement. EITI Board suspended Mali's membership
in April/May 2012. EITI reports covering 2011 data still published but new
process paused. Gold mining operations continued at major mines but revenue
transparency process formally suspended. Suspension due to governance violation.

sources.citation: EITI Mali suspended April-May 2012 post-coup; CNRDRE governance violation

2013.assessment:
Mali reinstated as EITI Candidate following elections and return to civilian
government in 2013. Elections in August 2013 and Keïta government allowed EITI
Board to reinstate Mali. Candidate status (not compliant) while rebuilding
multi-stakeholder group and reporting processes. EITI reports being rebuilt.
Gold mining revenue reconciliation process restarting. Transitional period
between suspension and return to full compliance.

sources.citation: EITI Mali reinstated as candidate 2013; post-elections; rebuilding reports

2014.assessment:
Mali continued rebuilding EITI compliance in 2014 as EITI Candidate. Reports
for years 2012-2013 being produced with gold revenue data. Multi-stakeholder
group re-established. IMF ECF program on track providing complementary fiscal
transparency pressure. Major gold mines (Syama, Loulo-Gounkoto, Fekola development)
participating. Working toward re-achieving full Compliant status.

sources.citation: EITI Mali candidate 2014; rebuilding 2012-2013 reports; MSG re-established

2015.assessment:
Mali undergoing EITI validation process in 2015 to re-achieve Compliant status.
EITI reports covering 2013-2014 gold revenues produced. Algiers Accord creating
institutional framework including resource revenue commitments. Multi-stakeholder
group active. Fekola mine (B2Gold) coming online adding new reporting requirements.
Mali expected to re-achieve compliance in 2015-2016 validation cycle.

sources.citation: EITI Mali validation 2015; 2013-2014 reports; Fekola new mine; compliance expected

2016.assessment:
Mali re-achieved EITI Compliant status (with corrective actions) in 2016.
EITI validation found substantial progress but identified areas needing improvement
— particularly beneficial ownership disclosure and completeness of revenues captured.
Gold mining sector transparency restored to pre-2012 levels. Fekola mine adding
new reconciliation requirements. IMF ECF on track. EITI reports for 2014-2015
fully produced. Compliant but with specific corrective action requirements.

sources.citation: EITI Mali Compliant with corrective actions 2016; validation found gaps

2017.assessment:
Mali maintained EITI Compliant status with corrective actions in 2017. Regular
EITI reports being produced. Fekola mine (B2Gold) now fully operational adding
significant gold production to reporting. G5 Sahel security spending creating
some revenue-extraction tensions not captured by EITI. MSG active. New EITI
Standard requirements (beneficial ownership, project-level reporting) creating
additional compliance demands. Reports for 2015-2016 produced.

sources.citation: EITI Mali compliant corrective actions 2017; Fekola operational; G5 tension

2018.assessment:
Mali maintained EITI Compliant with corrective actions in 2018. New EITI Standard
2019 introducing more demanding requirements. Validation identifying beneficial
ownership disclosure as persistent gap. Reports for 2016-2017 produced. Fekola
mine major reporting addition. Disputed elections creating some political
uncertainty around MSG participation. Government broadly committed to EITI process
as part of international engagement strategy.

sources.citation: EITI Mali compliant corrective actions 2018; beneficial ownership gap; Fekola; new Standard 2019

2019.assessment:
Mali maintained EITI Compliant with corrective actions status in 2019. EITI
Standard 2019 now in effect with demanding new requirements on contract disclosure,
beneficial ownership, and project-level reporting. Reports for 2017-2018 produced.
Gold mining sector disclosure maintained despite challenging security environment.
Pre-coup civilian government committed to EITI compliance as credibility signal
to international community.

sources.citation: EITI Mali compliant corrective actions 2019; EITI Standard 2019; gold sector maintained

2020.assessment:
Mali's EITI status threatened by August 2020 coup. EITI Board reviewing Mali's
status following governance change. COVID-19 also delaying EITI reporting processes
globally. Reports for 2018-2019 pending. Junta government uncertain commitment
to EITI standards. EITI Board threatening suspension but not yet fully suspending
in H2 2020. Status effectively downgraded to candidate or under review — the coup
broke the governance requirements for full compliance.

sources.citation: EITI Mali status threatened post-Aug 2020 coup; EITI Board review; COVID delays

2021.assessment:
Mali suspended from EITI following second coup (May 2021). EITI Board suspended
Mali's membership as the junta governance violated EITI requirements for civilian
democratic oversight of multi-stakeholder groups. EITI reports paused. Gold
mining revenue transparency formally suspended. Gold mines (Fekola, Loulo-Gounkoto,
Syama) continuing operations but EITI reconciliation process halted. Wagner
Group deployment creating new extractive revenue opacity concerns.

sources.citation: EITI Mali suspended 2021; second coup; EITI Board suspension; gold revenue opacity

2022.assessment:
Mali remained suspended from EITI in 2022. Junta governance continuing to violate
EITI democratic governance requirements. ECOWAS comprehensive sanctions January-
July 2022. French expulsion February 2022 removing key bilateral partner for
EITI support. Wagner Group operations creating significant extractive revenue
opacity — gold and other resource revenues potentially diverted to pay for Wagner
services outside any accountability framework. Worst extractive transparency
period since pre-EITI membership era.

sources.citation: EITI Mali suspended 2022; Wagner gold revenue opacity; French expelled; ECOWAS sanctions

Gold mining revenue transparency significantly reduced without EITI oversight. No meaningful extractive transparency mechanisms operating.

Gold mining revenue transparency significantly reduced without EITI oversight. No meaningful extractive transparency mechanisms operating.

No freedom of information legislation existed. The judiciary functioned with some independence but capacity and access limitations persisted. WJP did not cover Mali in this period.

2010.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index did not include Mali in its 2010 report. Qualitative
assessment based on contextual evidence. Mali was a functioning democracy under
President ATT with an active judiciary and National Assembly. Laws were published
in the Journal Officiel but with limited accessibility, especially outside Bamako.
Customary law widely applied alongside formal legal system. Access to justice
unequal — rural poor had limited access to courts. Estimated moderate legal
transparency with significant gaps, approximate WJP F3 equivalent ~43-45.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali legal transparency 2010; WJP pre-coverage

2011.assessment:
WJP did not include Mali in 2011. Pre-crisis year. Similar to 2010 — functional
legal framework under ATT democratic government. Laws published officially but
limited public accessibility. Judiciary operating normally. MNLA formation in
October 2011 had not yet disrupted legal institutions. Estimated legal transparency
comparable to 2010, approximately WJP F3 equivalent ~43-45.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali legal transparency 2011; WJP pre-coverage

2012.assessment:
WJP did not include Mali in 2012. Severe deterioration in legal transparency
following the March 2012 coup. CNRDRE junta suspended the constitution and ruled
by decree. Courts operating under military oversight. Northern Mali under jihadist
control replaced formal law with Sharia. Legal documents and official gazettes
not published normally. Estimated dramatic decline — approximate WJP F3 equivalent
~28-30, difficult/arbitrary enforcement territory.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali legal transparency 2012; constitution suspended; rule by decree

2013.assessment:
WJP did not include Mali in 2013 (first WJP Mali report in 2014). Partial recovery
following Operation Serval and August 2013 elections. Keïta government restored
constitutional order. Courts and National Assembly resumed normal operations.
Legal transparency improving from coup trough but still below pre-coup levels.
Northern Mali legal system still rebuilding. Estimated approximate WJP F3 ~36-38.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali legal transparency 2013; Keïta constitutional restoration

2014.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 (Open Government) score approximately 0.42 (stored as 42 per
value × 100 convention). Mali's first year in WJP coverage. Score in the
partial_significant_gaps range. Laws published officially but public accessibility
limited. Freedom of information mechanisms weak. Enforcement of laws inconsistent.
Civilian government restored legal order post-coup. Judiciary functioning but
underfunded and with weak independence. IMF ECF creating some governance improvement
pressure. Northern Mali legal system still normalizing.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2014; score ~0.42

2015.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.42 (stored 42) in 2015. Stable at 2014 level.
Algiers Accord (June 2015) providing institutional framework. Civilian government
maintaining legal order. Budget law and regulatory framework published but
with limited online accessibility. National Assembly active and legislative
transparency moderate. Courts functioning but with capacity limitations.
Overall partial transparency with significant access gaps.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2015; score ~0.42

2016.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.41 (stored 41) in 2016. Slight decline from
2015. Legal transparency stagnating. Government regulatory framework not improving
public accessibility. Security situation in growing areas limiting effective rule
of law beyond Bamako. Weak FOI mechanisms. Legislative processes maintained but
dominated by executive. Slight decline reflecting ongoing governance challenges.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2016; score ~0.41

2017.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.40 (stored 40) in 2017. Gradual decline
continuing. JNIM formation (March 2017) expanding areas outside formal legal
order. Government unable to enforce rule of law in large parts of central and
northern Mali. Legal framework publications maintained in Bamako. International
obligations met formally but implementation weak. G5 Sahel security spending
creating some off-budget, off-legal-framework expenditures.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2017; score ~0.40

2018.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.39 (stored 39) in 2018. Continued gradual
decline. Disputed elections (July-August 2018) undermined legal legitimacy
perceptions. Keïta re-elected amid opposition boycotts and fraud allegations.
Regulatory framework published but enforcement increasingly arbitrary especially
in areas with security force deployments. FOI law discussion but no implementation.
Legal transparency declining as political legitimacy contested.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2018; score ~0.39

2019.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.38 (stored 38) in 2019. Continuing slow
decline. Large areas of Mali effectively outside formal legal order (Kidal,
Ménaka, Gao, Mopti). M5-RFP political protests highlighting governance failures.
National Assembly functioning but legitimacy contested. Laws published in official
gazette but inaccessible in most of the country. Weak FOI mechanisms and
consultation processes. Pre-coup civilian government legal framework deteriorating.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2019; score ~0.38

2020.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.36 (stored 36) in 2020. Significant decline
following August 2020 coup. CNSP junta suspended the constitution, dissolved the
National Assembly, and governed by decree. Rule of law severely weakened. Legal
framework nominally maintained but civilian legal oversight removed. Courts
operating under de facto junta control. Laws and decrees issued without legislative
oversight. Major deterioration in legal transparency and open government.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2020; score ~0.36; coup dissolved NA

2021.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.35 (stored 35) in 2021. Further decline from
2020. Second coup (May 2021) deepened rule-by-decree governance. Goïta consolidated
power removing even transitional institutional checks. Transition charter replacing
constitutional framework. Laws and regulations issued without judicial or legislative
review. Wagner Group deployment creating parallel security-legal authority. IMF
program suspension removing international governance discipline. Legal transparency
at lowest point of civilian-equivalent period.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2021; score ~0.35; second coup deepened rule by decree

2022.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score approximately 0.34 (stored 34) in 2022. Continued deterioration.
Junta governance by decree continuing with no civilian legislative oversight.
French expulsion (February 2022) and break with Western legal assistance partners
reducing governance support. Wagner Group operating outside any legal accountability
framework. Moura massacre (March 2022) demonstrated impunity. ECOWAS comprehensive
sanctions January-July. Legal transparency at historic low — minimal rule by decree
rather than published legal framework. Decrees issued without public consultation.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Mali Factor 3 2022; score ~0.34; junta decree governance

Arbitrary enforcement by security forces. Civil society organizations and legal NGOs faced operational restrictions.

Some restrictions and occasional journalist harassment occurred, but Mali was considered one of West Africa's better press freedom environments.

2010.assessment:
Mali ranked approximately 26th out of 178 in the 2010 RSF Press Freedom Index —
one of the best rankings in Africa and globally notable for a low-income country.
RSF inverted score approximately 71. Mali under ATT government had a vibrant
independent press — over 100 radio stations, multiple private newspapers, and
active TV channels. Journalists could criticize the government. Press freedom
was considered a democratic achievement. High freedom in West African context.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index Mali 2010; ranked ~26/178

2011.assessment:
Mali maintained excellent press freedom in 2011. RSF inverted score approximately 72.
Mali ranked around 25th globally — consistently near the top in sub-Saharan Africa.
MNLA formation in October 2011 had not yet impacted press environment. Active
private radio stations, newspapers, and online media. Journalists reporting freely
on political affairs, corruption. Pre-crisis baseline satisfactory environment.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index Mali 2011; ranked ~25/179

2012.assessment:
Severe press freedom deterioration in 2012. RSF inverted score approximately 51.
The March 2012 coup saw CNRDRE junta temporarily shut down media, detain journalists,
and occupy the national broadcaster ORTM. In northern Mali, jihadist groups (AQIM,
Ansar Dine, MUJAO) imposed Sharia bans on music broadcasting and expelled or
threatened journalists. Reporters could not freely operate in the north. Several
journalists fled the country. International correspondents had limited access.
Dramatic fall from satisfactory to difficult category.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2012; coup media shutdown; northern jihadist press ban

2013.assessment:
Partial press freedom recovery in 2013. RSF inverted score approximately 57.
Operation Serval restored major northern cities, removing jihadist media restrictions.
Elections in August 2013 reinvigorated political media coverage. However, security
situation in north and center created dangerous operating environment for journalists.
Several journalists remained at risk from jihadist groups. Some self-censorship
on security topics. Modest improvement from 2012 crisis trough.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2013; partial recovery post-Serval; security remains dangerous for journalists

2014.assessment:
Press freedom remained problematic in 2014. RSF inverted score approximately 53.
Civilian government allowed media plurality in Bamako but insecurity in north and
expanding center limited reporting. Kidal crisis (May 2014) created no-go zones.
Journalists faced threats from armed groups operating across the country. AQIM and
MUJAO sporadic threats. Some journalists self-censoring on sensitive topics.
Still well below pre-coup levels.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2014; insecurity limiting reporting; Kidal no-go zones

2015.assessment:
Press freedom slightly improved in 2015. RSF inverted score approximately 55.
Algiers Accord (June 2015) created diplomatic context. Bamako press remained
active and pluralistic. November 2015 Radisson Blu attack showed vulnerability
of Bamako to jihadist violence, creating press chilling effects. Northern and
central Mali remained dangerous for field reporting. RSF classified Mali as
difficult environment overall.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2015; Algiers Accord; Radisson attack chilling effect; north/center dangerous

2016.assessment:
Press freedom slightly declining in 2016. RSF inverted score approximately 52.
Violence spreading from north to central Mali (Mopti region) created new dangerous
reporting zones. JNIM (formed March 2017) predecessor groups increasing attacks.
International journalists increasingly restricted in Mali outside Bamako. Some
Malian journalists reporting on security received threats. Bamako media scene
active but national reporting increasingly dangerous.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2016; Mopti spreading violence; international journalists restricted outside Bamako

2017.assessment:
Press freedom declining in 2017. RSF inverted score approximately 50. JNIM
formation (March 2017) intensified armed group presence across Mali. Large parts
of the country effectively inaccessible to journalists. Central Mali (Mopti)
reporting extremely dangerous. Bamako media remained relatively free but
self-censorship on security matters increasing. Government beginning to restrict
coverage of security operations citing military information.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2017; JNIM; large areas inaccessible; Mopti extremely dangerous

2018.assessment:
Press freedom continuing to decline in 2018. RSF inverted score approximately 48.
Disputed election environment (July-August 2018) created political pressure on media.
Government and Keïta camp accused opposition-sympathetic media of bias. Security
environment in Mopti/Ségou creating mass journalist no-go zones. International
organizations raising alerts about journalist safety across Mali. Bamako independent
media still operating but under increasing political pressure.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2018; disputed elections political pressure; journalist no-go zones expanding

2019.assessment:
Press freedom continuing slow decline in 2019. RSF inverted score approximately 47.
Large portions of Mali inaccessible to local and international journalists. Ogossagou
massacre (March 2019) reporting highlighted dangers — journalists risked armed group
attacks. Government restricting some security-related reporting. M5-RFP political
protest movement generated some political media pressure. Pre-coup civilian
government broadly allowing Bamako media freedom.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2019; large areas inaccessible; Ogossagou reporting dangerous; political pressure from protests

2020.assessment:
Sharp press freedom deterioration in 2020. RSF inverted score approximately 37.
August 2020 coup led by Goïta/CNSP immediately restricted media — ORTM placed
under junta control, some private media suspended temporarily. Several journalists
reporting on the coup received threats. RFI (Radio France Internationale) briefly
suspended. M5-RFP protests coverage restricted. Junta era began systematic
intimidation of critical journalists. Sharp decline into very_serious territory.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2020; coup Aug 2020; ORTM seized; RFI suspended; journalist intimidation

2021.assessment:
Continued deterioration of press freedom in 2021. RSF inverted score approximately 34.
Second coup (May 2021) by Goïta deepened media restrictions. Junta government
increasingly hostile to critical journalists. RFI suspended again. Journalists
covering protests or corruption faced arrest risks. Several arrests of journalists
reported. Wagner Group negotiations created new off-limits reporting topics.
Press freedom now firmly in very_serious category — one of the worst environments
in West Africa.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2021; second coup; RFI suspended; journalist arrests; Wagner off-limits

2022.assessment:
Severe press freedom in 2022. RSF inverted score approximately 30. France expelled
February 2022 — French-language media and RFI suspended permanently. TV5 Monde
suspended. Junta decreed prohibition on broadcasting reports contradicting military
official accounts. Wagner Group operations completely off-limits. Moura massacre
(March 2022) reporting suppressed — international journalists denied access and
UN investigation obstructed. Most international media could not operate freely.
Malian journalists faced arrest for critical reporting. Worst press freedom
period in Mali's recorded history.

sources.citation: RSF Mali 2022; RFI + TV5 suspended; France expelled; Moura reporting suppressed; Wagner off-limits

2023.assessment: Press freedom in Mali severely deteriorated in 2023 under military junta. Multiple journalists expelled (including French media). BBC and RFI Afrique suspended. Local journalists face arrest and harassment. RSF ranked Mali very low. Critical reporting on the junta or Wagner Group led to detentions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2023, Mali

2024.assessment: Press freedom in Mali severely deteriorated in 2024 under military junta. Multiple journalists expelled (including French media). BBC and RFI Afrique suspended. Local journalists face arrest and harassment. RSF ranked Mali very low. Critical reporting on the junta or Wagner Group led to detentions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024, Mali

2025.assessment: Press freedom in Mali severely deteriorated in 2025 under military junta. Multiple journalists expelled (including French media). BBC and RFI Afrique suspended. Local journalists face arrest and harassment. RSF ranked Mali very low. Critical reporting on the junta or Wagner Group led to detentions.

2010.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2010. Mali had moderate statistical capacity —
above-average for low-income sub-Saharan African countries. The Direction Nationale
de la Statistique et de l'Informatique (DNSI) had been receiving international
support to improve data collection. National accounts, demographic surveys (DHS),
and household budget surveys conducted. IMF Article IV consultations maintained
regular data provision. Reasonable base for a low-income West African state.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2010; score ~67; DNSI operational; DHS surveys; IMF data provision

2011.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2011. Slight improvement from 2010. Statistical
capacity stable under ATT government. National statistical office continuing regular
data production. Census preparations underway. International aid supporting
statistical capacity building. Pre-crisis peak year for statistical systems.
MNLA formation in October 2011 had not yet impacted statistical operations.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2011; score ~68; pre-crisis peak; census preparations; aid support

2012.assessment:
WB SCI score dropped to approximately 57 in 2012. The coup (March 2012) and loss
of northern territories severely disrupted statistical operations. DNSI capacity
reduced as government functions collapsed. IMF program suspension halted data
review processes. Northern Mali data collection completely impossible under jihadist
control. National accounts produced with significant gaps. International aid
for statistics suspended. Major deterioration from 2011 pre-crisis level.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2012; score ~57; coup disrupted; north inaccessible; IMF suspended

2013.assessment:
WB SCI score recovered to approximately 60 in 2013. Operation Serval restored
security in major northern cities, allowing partial resumption of data collection.
Civilian government under Keïta restored DNSI operations. International statistical
aid beginning to resume. IMF ECF negotiations requiring economic data provision.
Some northern areas still inaccessible. Partial recovery toward pre-coup levels.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2013; score ~60; post-Serval recovery; DNSI restored; IMF ECF requiring data

2014.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 63 in 2014. Continued recovery in statistical capacity.
IMF ECF program on track requiring regular data provision. International donors
resuming statistical capacity building assistance. National accounts improving.
DHS survey data being processed. Kidal crisis creating some northern gaps but
central and southern data collection normalized.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2014; score ~63; IMF ECF requiring data; donors resuming stats aid

2015.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 65 in 2015. Statistical capacity stabilizing in moderate-
good range. Algiers Accord (June 2015) created framework for national reconciliation
including institutional rebuilding. IMF ECF maintaining data discipline. National
statistical office receiving capacity building support from World Bank and PARIS21.
Household survey data being collected in accessible areas. Northern data gaps remain.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2015; score ~65; IMF ECF; WB/PARIS21 capacity support; Algiers Accord

2016.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 65 in 2016. Stable statistical capacity. IMF ECF
on track maintaining data reporting requirements. Central and southern Mali data
collection normal. Violence spreading from north to Mopti creating new survey
access difficulties in central Mali. National accounts maintained. Overall
statistical system stable despite security challenges.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2016; score ~65; stable; Mopti access difficulties; IMF on track

2017.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 64 in 2017. Slight decline as JNIM formation (March 2017)
began expanding violence into central Mali, creating more statistical survey no-go
zones. Data collection in Mopti and Ségou regions becoming difficult. IMF ECF
maintaining national accounts. Bamako-based statistical operations continuing.
Growing data gaps in conflict-affected areas.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2017; score ~64; JNIM expanding survey no-go zones; data gaps growing

2018.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 63 in 2018. Continued modest decline. Data collection
in central Mali (Mopti, Ségou) increasingly difficult. Disputed elections created
some reporting delays. National statistical office maintained core functions.
IMF ECF requiring data provision supporting macro data quality. Household survey
coverage shrinking due to security no-go zones.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2018; score ~63; central Mali data gaps; elections delays; IMF ECF data

2019.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 62 in 2019. Gradual slow decline continuing. Growing
areas of central Mali inaccessible for surveys. Ogossagou massacre (March 2019)
created major displacement making data collection in affected area impossible.
IMF ECF maintaining national account discipline. Bamako statistical operations
normal. Nationally representative data increasingly difficult to achieve.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2019; score ~62; Ogossagou displacement; survey coverage declining; IMF ECF

2020.assessment:
WB SCI score approximately 59 in 2020 — last year of SCI publication. COVID-19
disrupted normal survey operations. August 2020 coup created governance disruption
affecting statistical office functions. IMF program suspended removing key data
discipline mechanism. SCI discontinued after 2020. Score reflects deteriorating
coverage, COVID disruption, and coup impact on statistical institutional capacity.

sources.citation: WB SCI Mali 2020; score ~59; final SCI year; COVID disruption; coup Aug 2020; IMF suspended

2021.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued after 2020; no score available for 2021. Qualitative assessment
suggests continued deterioration. Second coup (May 2021) deepened governance disruption.
IMF program remained suspended. ECOWAS sanctions limiting international statistical
cooperation. Security preventing surveys in large parts of Mali. Junta government
less invested in international data reporting standards. Statistical capacity
estimated at moderate or below moderate level (~55-58).

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali statistical capacity 2021; SCI discontinued

2022.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued; no score available for 2022. Statistical capacity likely
declined further. Wagner Group presence and French expulsion (February 2022) created
institutional disruption. ECOWAS comprehensive sanctions (January-July 2022) limiting
international cooperation. Large parts of Mali inaccessible for surveys. National
accounts data production continuing but with larger uncertainty. Moura massacre
(March 2022) data collection impossible in affected areas. IMF program still
suspended removing data discipline requirements. Estimated poor to moderate range (~50-55).

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment Mali statistical capacity 2022; SCI discontinued; Wagner; sanctions

2023.assessment: Statistical capacity in Mali likely declined in 2023. INSTAT faced reduced donor support, conflict disruption of data collection in northern/central regions, and general governance deterioration under junta. Many statistical surveys could not cover conflict-affected areas.
